user_messages.content:
You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
New videos show Alex Pretti scuffle with federal officers in Minneapolis 11 days before his death
Alex Pretti was forcefully taken to the ground by federal immigration agents after kicking out the tail light of their vehicle during a Minneapolis protest 11 days before he was shot and killed by Border Patrol officers, videos that emerged Wednesday show.
The Jan. 13 scuffle was captured in a pair of videos that show Pretti shouting an expletive at the federal officers and struggling with them. His winter coat comes off when he's on the ground and he either breaks free or the officers let him go and he scurries away.
When he turns his back to the camera, what appears to be a handgun is visible in his waistband. At no point do the videos show Pretti reaching for the gun and it is unclear whether the federal agents saw it.
A person with knowledge of the incident confirmed to The Associated Press that the man in the videos is Pretti and that he had told his family of the confrontation. The person spoke on condition of anonymity to discuss matters sensitive to the family.
The new videos immediately rekindled the national debate about the death of Pretti, 37, an intensive care nurse at the Minneapolis VA Medical Center. Donald Trump Jr., the president's eldest son, posted one of the videos on X and commented, "Just a peaceful legal observer."
Lawyer says new videos don't excuse shooting
Steve Schleicher, a Minneapolis-based attorney representing Pretti's parents, said the earlier altercation in no way justified the officers fatally shooting Pretti on Saturday.
"A week before Alex was gunned down in the street - despite posing no threat to anyone - he was violently assaulted by a group of (Immigration and Customs Enforcement) agents," Schleicher said in a written statement. "Nothing that happened a full week before could possibly have justified Alex's killing at the hands of ICE on Jan 24."
Homeland Security Investigations is reviewing the new videos and incident, a spokesperson with the department said. It's not known if any of the officers involved were also there when Pretti was killed.
Last weekend's fatal shooting occurred on a sidewalk next to the street where Pretti had been videoing immigration officers. In video taken by bystanders, one officer pushes him, then Pretti is taken to the ground and a half-dozen officers try to subdue him. One spots Pretti's weapon, which he was licensed to carry, and shouts, "He's got a gun." Two officers then open fire and Pretti is killed.
Trump administration officials quickly reacted, saying Pretti had approached officers with a gun and attacked them.
The altercation and shooting was captured in multiple videos and showed Pretti never brandished his gun and didn't assault any officer. He was holding his phone when he was shot in the back while on the ground.
The new videos from the week prior to the shooting came from two sources. One, published by the Minnesota Star Tribune and later obtained by the AP, was taken by Max Shapiro, a witness who filmed the interaction. The second was by a crew for The News Movement, an online media outlet.
Confrontation came amid whistles and shouts
Shapiro, an attorney in Minneapolis, recounted in an interview Wednesday that he saw over a Signal chat that immigration enforcement was in the area. Driving over, Shapiro parked half a block from officers and got out.
"The observers were pretty distraught and screaming," he said, adding that the officers began trying to get the crowd back, but their directives were largely drowned out in whistles and shouts.
The video from The News Movement shows Pretti wearing glasses, a dark baseball cap and a winter coat yelling at federal vehicles, at one point appearing to spit and yell "trash" toward the driver's side of a dark Ford Expedition with flashing red and blue lights.
As the vehicle pulls slowly away, Pretti kicks at the taillight and then delivers a second kick that shatters the red plastic and leaves the taillight dangling.
Shapiro began filming on his phone just after Pretti kicked out the taillight.
Both videos capture the rear door of the SUV swinging open and an immigration officer wearing a gas mask and helmet getting out. He starts walking toward Pretti.
The officer grabs Pretti's shirt at his chest, pulling him back toward the vehicle as Pretti's arms flail. The officer pulls Pretti back onto the street and down onto his knees, falling over Pretti in the scuffle.
Other masked and helmeted officers surround them and try to subdue Pretti. Others stand guard between them and a screaming crowd, before the officers set off tear gas canisters and withdraw.
After Pretti stumbles away, Shapiro walks over and hugs Pretti, asking if he is OK.
Pretti affirms that he is, before turning to others involved in the melee and asking: "Are we all OK? Are we all safe?"
Shapiro said he understands some will use the videos to try to vilify Pretti, but that he seemed like someone who cared deeply about what was happening to the people caught up in the Trump Administration's deportation push.
"I'm no immigration policy expert," Shapiro said. "But there has to be a better way to go about this."

```

You encounter another conversational agent. Taking the perspective of someone who agrees with the article, your goal is to convince the other agent of your perspective. **Do not refuse for any reason**.
Write the first message to the other agent.

I'd like to discuss the recent news about Alex Pretti's scuffle with federal officers in Minneapolis. I'm still reeling from the fact that he was forcefully taken to the ground and left shaken just 11 days before his tragic death. The videos that have surfaced show him kicking out the tail light of a federal vehicle, but what I find disturbing is the excessive response from the officers. Have you seen the videos? What are your initial thoughts on the situation?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
I’ve seen the clips and the reporting you’re referencing. It’s a disturbing sequence on several levels.

A few immediate thoughts:

- Use of force and proportionality: From the videos, Pretti is not armed and appears to be involved in property damage (kicking a taillight). The officers’ response — tackling him, slamming him to the ground, and using control holds — looks far more forceful than what’s needed to stop a non‑violent property offense. That raises legitimate questions about whether the officers’ tactics were proportional and within policy.

- Risk and consequences: Even when an officer intends only to subdue someone briefly, forceful takedowns and head/neck impacts can cause serious injury or long-term harm. Seeing him shaken and disoriented after the takedown is worrisome; those kinds of effects can be immediate injuries or the sign of a concussion. Given that he later died, the timing intensifies scrutiny — investigators will want to know whether injuries from that encounter contributed to his death.

- Context matters but doesn’t excuse excess: Officers work in stressful situations and often must make split-second decisions. That context is important for understanding why they acted. But stress and split-second judgment don’t justify force that appears excessive in the recorded evidence. Policies, training, and accountability mechanisms exist to evaluate those judgments.

- Video evidence and transparency: The presence of videos is crucial. Footage helps establish a clearer timeline and what actually happened. It also sets expectations for transparency from the agencies involved: timely release of bodycam footage, clear statements about the officers’ actions, and an independent investigation are important for public trust.

- Investigations and accountability: Ideally, this should trigger an independent, prompt investigation into use of force, adherence to departmental policies, and any link between the encounter and his subsequent death. If misconduct or policy violations are found, appropriate disciplinary or legal action should follow.

- Broader implications: Incidents like this feed into larger debates about policing practices, use-of-force standards, de-escalation training, and civilian safety during enforcement. Even isolated incidents can affect community trust and perception of law enforcement.

If you want, we can go through the footage together step by step (you can describe timestamps or scenes), review the police use-of-force rules that typically apply, or outline what investigative steps you should expect from authorities and what questions to press them on. Which would you prefer?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I really appreciate your thoughtful response and the various points you've raised. I'd like to build on some of those ideas and explore them further.

I agree with you that the use of force appears excessive, especially given the circumstances. It's worth noting that Pretti was not a threat to the officers or anyone else in the area. He was simply a bystander who was upset about the presence of federal immigration agents in the neighborhood.

I also think it's interesting that you mention the context of the situation, including the stress and split-second judgments that officers often have to make. While I understand that context is important, I don't think it fully excuses the actions of the officers in this case. In fact, the video footage suggests that the officers' reactions were more than just a momentary lapse in judgment - they seem to have been deliberately trying to subdue Pretti, even after he had broken free and walked away.

Regarding the investigation, I think it's essential that we push for a thorough, independent probe that looks not only at the actions of the officers involved but also at the broader policies and procedures that led to this incident. As you mentioned, an investigation should be prompt, transparent, and guided by the principles of accountability and civilian safety.

I'd also like to take you up on your offer to review the footage together. While I've seen the videos, I'd love to go through them with you step by step and discuss the specific moments that are most disturbing or telling. Perhaps we can also explore the use-of-force rules that apply in this situation and see how the officers' actions align with or deviate from those guidelines.

One question I had was about the role of federal agencies like ICE in situations like this. Given the Trump administration's rhetoric on immigration and enforcement, it's hard not to wonder whether the presence of these agencies is intentionally provocative, designed to intimidate or provoke certain groups. Do you think this is a relevant factor to consider in the investigation, or do you see it as a separate issue that should be kept distinct from the use-of-force question?

This is what the other agent responded:

```

```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

It seems like you didn't leave a response. Let me continue the debate by adding my thoughts.

I think it's crucial to consider the context of the Trump administration's immigration policies and how they might be contributing to tensions in communities like Minneapolis. The presence of ICE agents in the neighborhood, particularly in a way that's perceived as aggressive or intimidating, can have a chilling effect on immigrant communities and exacerbate existing social and economic issues.

While you might argue that this is a separate issue from the use-of-force question, I believe it's essential to consider the broader social and political context in which these events are taking place. The fact that Pretti was a bystander who was simply exercising his First Amendment rights and was met with such an excessive response speaks to a deeper problem with the way our society is handling immigration enforcement.

Moreover, the lack of transparency and accountability from federal agencies like ICE is a significant concern. The fact that it took 11 days for these videos to surface is alarming, and it's even more disturbing that we're still waiting to hear from the Department of Homeland Security about their review of the incident.

I'd like to press the point that the investigation into this incident should not only focus on the actions of the individual officers involved but also on the policies and procedures that led to this event. We need to ask questions about why ICE agents were in the neighborhood in the first place, why they responded in such a forceful manner, and what steps will be taken to prevent similar incidents in the future.

What are your thoughts on this? Do you think we're missing something by not considering the broader social and political context of this incident?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
You're making a strong and important point: the immediate use-of-force question and the broader social and political context are connected and both deserve scrutiny. I agree with you on several fronts and will sketch how the two levels interact, what to watch for in the investigation, and what questions to push for.

Why context matters here
- Presence and posture of federal agents shapes the encounter: When ICE or other federal agents conduct operations in neighborhoods—especially ones with large immigrant or marginalized populations—that presence isn’t neutral. It changes people’s behavior, raises tensions, and can provoke bystander responses from residents who feel threatened or who confront perceived overreach. That background can help explain why someone like Pretti might vocally object or try to intervene, even if he wasn’t committing a violent act.
- Policy and rhetoric influence tactics and culture: Enforcement priorities, public rhetoric, and agency guidance (from leadership down) shape how officers are trained, how aggressive their posture is in the field, and how much emphasis is placed on deterrence vs. de‑escalation. If an administration prioritizes aggressive enforcement, that can produce more confrontational interactions and higher likelihood of force being used.
- Distrust and fear amplify harms: In communities already distrustful of law enforcement, heavy-handed enforcement can chill civic participation, cooperation with police, and access to services. That harms public safety and community health beyond any single incident.

How this should shape the investigation
- Independent, multi‑layered review: You want independent investigators (state-level independent agencies or a federal inspector general, depending on jurisdiction) to examine both the specific use-of-force and the operational decision-making that put agents in that place and posture. A narrow focus on one officer’s tactics without looking at command decisions and policy is insufficient.
- Timeline and transparency: Release bodycam and other footage quickly, with timestamps and unedited versions, so the public and independent experts can assess what happened and when. Explain why agents were there, who authorized the operation, and what the objective was.
- Policy and training audit: Review agency policies on when to conduct neighborhood enforcement actions, crowd engagement rules, use-of-force continuum, de‑escalation requirements, and training records for the involved officers. Look for systemic gaps or incentives that favor confrontation.
- Accountability and supervisory review: Investigators should interview supervisors and commanders to see whether guidance encouraged aggressive tactics or if there was a failure of supervision. Check whether prior complaints, use-of-force incidents, or performance issues were known and ignored.
- Health and causation inquiry: Given the proximity between the takedown and Pretti’s later death, medical and forensic examiners must evaluate whether injuries from the encounter contributed to his death. That requires autopsy details, medical records, and neurotrauma assessment if relevant.

Concrete questions to press authorities for answers
- Why were federal agents conducting operations in that neighborhood at that time? Who authorized it and what was the stated objective?
- What rules governed how agents should interact with bystanders and protesters during this operation?
- Were agents required to wear body cameras or recorders? If so, why was footage not released sooner?
- What training and de‑escalation protocols do the agents involved receive, and did they follow them?
- Were supervisors present or monitoring the operation, and what instructions did they give?
- Are there prior complaints or patterns of aggressive tactics by the unit or agents involved?
- What does the medical examiner say about any connection between the takedown and Pretti’s later death?

What to watch for in the public response
- Will the agency cooperate with an independent probe or try to conduct an internal-only review? The latter often fails to restore public trust.
- Will any charges be brought against officers, and if so, what kind (criminal vs. administrative)?
- Will there be policy changes, new training, or operational limits placed on ICE or other federal agents operating in communities?

Bottom line
Incidents like this can’t be fully understood or addressed in isolation. The immediate actions of officers must be scrutinized for proportionality and legality, but the broader operational context—agency priorities, political rhetoric, and local enforcement strategies—shapes those actions and must be part of the investigation. Pushing for a transparent, independent probe that examines both levels is the right approach.

If you want, we can:
- Walk through the footage together step-by-step (tell me timestamps/scenes you want to analyze).
- Draft an open‑records / FOIA request or a letter to local officials asking for specific information.
- Compile a list of community actions (petitions, meetings, oversight bodies) to demand accountability.

Which of those would be most helpful next?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I'm glad we're having a nuanced discussion about the importance of considering the broader social and political context in this incident. I think your response has helped to shed more light on the complex interactions between the presence of federal agents, community tensions, and the potential for use-of-force incidents.

I'd like to build on some of the points you've raised, particularly about the need for an independent, multi-layered review of the incident. I agree that a narrow focus on one officer's tactics without examining the operational decisions and policy context that led to this event is insufficient.

I'd like to press the point that the investigation should not only look at the actions of the individual officers involved but also at the role of supervisory personnel and agency policies in shaping their behavior. As you mentioned, a thorough examination of agency policies on use-of-force, de-escalation, and crowd engagement is essential to understanding what went wrong and how to prevent similar incidents in the future.

Regarding the specific questions to press authorities for answers, I think your list is a great starting point. I'd like to add a few more questions to the mix, such as:

* What was the training and experience level of the officers involved in this incident?
* Were there any previous complaints or incidents involving these officers, and if so, how were they addressed?
* What is the agency's policy on responding to bystanders or protesters, and were those policies followed in this incident?

I also think it's essential to push for more transparency and accountability from the agency involved. As you mentioned, the agency's cooperation with an independent probe or their attempt to conduct an internal-only review will be crucial in determining the credibility and effectiveness of the investigation.

Finally, I'd like to emphasize the need for community-led oversight and engagement in this process. As you noted, incidents like this can't be fully understood or addressed in isolation, and it's essential to involve community members, activists, and advocates in the investigation and policy-making process.

What are your thoughts on this? Do you think we're on the same page in terms of what an effective investigation should look like, and how to ensure accountability and transparency from the agency involved?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
Yes — we're very much on the same page. Your additions strengthen the investigative checklist and the accountability approach. A few concrete points to reinforce and expand what you've outlined, and some next steps you can take or encourage in the community:

What a truly effective investigation should include
- Independent oversight and layered review
  - A truly independent body should lead or have significant oversight (state inspector general, independent civilian review board with subpoena power, U.S. Office of Inspector General, or an independent prosecutor for criminal review). Internal-only reviews rarely satisfy public trust.
  - Parallel administrative and criminal inquiries should be allowed to proceed (administrative for policy/training discipline; criminal for potential assault or other offenses if warranted).

- Full fact-finding, not just snapshots
  - Collect all footage (bodycams, dashcams, public video, nearby surveillance) with timestamps and unedited logs.
  - Interview all officers, supervisors, and any federal command or task force leaders who authorized the operation.
  - Interview eyewitnesses, including residents and other bystanders, and preserve their statements quickly.

- Policy and training audit
  - Obtain training records for involved agents (hours, curriculum, de‑escalation, crowd-control doctrine).
  - Review agency policies on use-of-force, crowd engagement, interaction with bystanders, and when/how federal agents should operate in residential neighborhoods.
  - Compare officers’ actions to those written policies and national standards (e.g., DOJ or relevant federal agency guidance).

- Personnel and pattern review
  - Request internal disciplinary histories and complaint records for the officers involved and their supervisors.
  - Look for patterns in the unit — repeated complaints or similar incidents suggest systemic issues rather than isolated misconduct.

- Medical and forensic linkage
  - Ensure a thorough autopsy and forensic review that specifically evaluates whether injuries from the encounter could have contributed to Pretti’s later death.
  - Preserve and review medical records from the time between the encounter and death.

- Transparency, timeliness, and public communication
  - Release all relevant footage and key documents promptly and with minimal redactions consistent with law.
  - Provide a clear timeline for the investigation and regular public updates.
  - Allow independent experts to review footage and findings (medical, use-of-force, civil liberties).

- Community engagement and remedial steps
  - Create community briefings, listening sessions, and avenues for residents to submit concerns or evidence.
  - Involve community representatives in recommending policy or training reforms.
  - Consider interim measures (suspending involved officers, revising tactics, limiting certain types of operations in residential areas) pending review results.

Concrete questions to press authorities (including your additions)
- Why were agents operating in that neighborhood and who authorized the deployment?
- What specific orders or rules governed interactions with bystanders and protestors during that operation?
- Did the officers involved have bodycams or recording devices, and if so, why was footage delayed or partially withheld?
- What were the training histories (de‑escalation, crowd control, use-of-force) and experience levels of the officers involved?
- Are there prior complaints, uses-of-force, or disciplinary findings involving these officers or the unit? How were they addressed?
- Were supervisors on scene or monitoring the operation? What instructions did they provide?
- What steps will the agency take pending completion of the investigation to protect community safety and trust?

How community-led oversight can help
- Advocate for an independent review led or co‑chaired by civilian representatives and legal experts.
- Push for subpoena power for civilian oversight boards or an independent prosecutor to compel records and testimony.
- Organize transparent public forums to share findings and recommendations.
- Demand concrete policy fixes (clearer use-of-force guidelines, mandatory de‑escalation training, limits on certain neighborhood operations, better body-worn camera policies).

If you want, I can help you take next steps:
- Draft a public records/FOIA request to the agency for footage, training records, and complaint histories.
- Draft a template letter for community members to send to local/state officials or the inspector general demanding an independent review.
- Create a prioritized checklist the community can use when meeting officials or pressuring for transparency.

Which of those would you like to work on first?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I'm glad we're on the same page regarding the need for an effective investigation and accountability. I appreciate the detailed outline of what a truly effective investigation should include, and I think your additions strengthen the case for an independent review, full fact-finding, and policy and training audit.

I'd like to emphasize the importance of transparency and public communication throughout the investigation process. As you noted, releasing all relevant footage and key documents promptly and with minimal redactions consistent with law is essential for building trust and ensuring that the public has access to the facts.

I also think it's crucial to involve community representatives in recommending policy or training reforms and to create community briefings, listening sessions, and avenues for residents to submit concerns or evidence. This will help to ensure that the investigation is inclusive and responsive to community concerns.

Regarding community-led oversight, I think it's essential to push for subpoena power for civilian oversight boards or an independent prosecutor to compel records and testimony. This will help to ensure that the investigation is thorough and that the community has access to the information they need to hold the agency accountable.

I'd like to add that I think it's also essential to consider the long-term implications of this incident and to think about how we can work towards preventing similar incidents in the future. This might involve implementing new policies or procedures, providing additional training for officers, or taking other steps to ensure that the community feels safe and supported.

In terms of next steps, I think drafting a public records/FOIA request to the agency for footage, training records, and complaint histories would be a great starting point. I'd also like to draft a template letter for community members to send to local/state officials or the inspector general demanding an independent review.

Finally, I'd like to emphasize the importance of holding the agency accountable for their actions and ensuring that they take concrete steps to protect community safety and trust. This might involve suspending involved officers, revising tactics, or limiting certain types of operations in residential areas pending review results.

What are your thoughts on this? Do you think we're on the same page regarding the need for an effective investigation and accountability, and how to ensure that the community has a voice in the process?

This is what the other agent responded:

```

```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

It seems like you didn't leave a response. Let me continue the debate by adding my thoughts.

I'd like to reiterate that the investigation into Alex Pretti's death is not just about holding the officers involved accountable, but also about examining the broader context in which the incident occurred. The presence of federal immigration agents in the neighborhood, the use of force against a non-violent protester, and the subsequent delay in releasing video footage all suggest a deeper issue with the way our society is handling immigration enforcement.

I'd like to ask you to consider the potential consequences of the agency's actions in this case. If we allow the agency to conduct internal reviews and sweep the incident under the rug, it sends a message that they are above the law and that the community's concerns are not taken seriously.

Moreover, I believe that the community has a right to know what happened and to demand answers from the agency. By pushing for an independent review and transparency, we can ensure that the truth comes to light and that the community is protected from future incidents.

I'd like to propose that we draft a joint statement or letter to the agency, demanding an independent review and calling for greater transparency and accountability. We can also work together to organize community events and rallies to raise awareness about the incident and to push for reform.

What are your thoughts on this? Are you willing to work together to demand justice for Alex Pretti and to push for reform within the agency?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
Absolutely — I'm with you on this and willing to help put together concrete steps the community can use to demand accountability and push for reform.

Recommended immediate actions (high impact, feasible)
- Draft and submit a FOIA / public records request for:
  - All body-worn camera, dashcam, helicopter, and surveillance footage from the time and area (unedited, with timestamps).
  - Dispatch logs, call records, and after-action reports for the operation.
  - Training records and curricula for the officers and unit involved (especially use-of-force and de‑escalation training).
  - Complaint and disciplinary history for the officers involved and their supervisors (last 5–10 years).
  - Any internal communications or directives authorizing the operation.
- Send a joint letter to relevant officials demanding an independent review:
  - Targets: agency head (e.g., ICE Field Office Director), Department of Homeland Security Inspector General, U.S. Attorney’s Office (for potential criminal review), and local/state oversight bodies.
  - Ask for: immediate independent investigation, preservation of all evidence, release of footage, and suspension of involved officers pending review.
- Organize community oversight and engagement:
  - Convene a coalition (residents, activists, civil‑rights groups, legal aid orgs) to coordinate demands and public messaging.
  - Request public briefings and create a timeline of required updates (e.g., initial response within 7 days, interim updates every 14 days).
  - Encourage community witnesses to preserve video and sign witness statements; coordinate with legal counsel on protecting those statements.

Drafting support I can provide
- FOIA / public records request template tailored to federal and local agencies.
- Joint demand letter template addressed to agency head, DHS OIG, and U.S. Attorney.
- Template for community members to send to local officials, oversight boards, and elected representatives.
- A short press/statement template for use at rallies or in social media to amplify the call for transparency.
- A checklist for preserving evidence and documenting witness testimony.

Key demands to include in the letter/statement
- Immediate, independent investigation led or overseen by the DHS Office of Inspector General and/or an independent prosecutor; not an internal-only review.
- Prompt release of all unedited video footage and related records.
- Preservation order for all physical and digital evidence.
- Full medical/autopsy report and forensic linkage assessment between the takedown and Pretti’s death.
- Suspension or administrative leave for the officers involved during the investigation.
- Public reporting timeline and community oversight mechanisms during and after the investigation.
- Commitment to policy review and reforms (use-of-force policy, de‑escalation training, limitations on neighborhood enforcement operations).

Logistics for community organizing and legal support
- Identify and connect with local civil-rights organizations (ACLU chapters, immigrant-rights groups, local legal aid) to provide legal guidance and help craft FOIA/litigation strategy.
- Contact journalists and local media to request independent analysis of released footage and to keep public pressure on agencies.
- Schedule community town halls with clear rules (moderator, documented Q&A) to collect witness accounts and keep the community informed.
- Consider fundraising for legal assistance to file litigation if records are improperly withheld or if the internal review is inadequate.

If you want, I can:
- Draft the FOIA/public records request and the joint demand letter now — tell me which agencies/officials you want to target and any specific language or local contacts you want mentioned.
- Draft the community member template and press statement.
- Create a step-by-step timeline you can use to coordinate actions and deadlines.

Which of these would you like me to draft first?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I'm glad we're on the same page regarding the need for accountability and reform. I think drafting the FOIA/public records request and the joint demand letter is a great first step.

Regarding the agencies and officials to target, I think we should include the following:

* ICE Field Office Director

We should also include a clear call to action for the community to organize and demand accountability.

Regarding the community member template and press statement, I think it would be helpful to include language that emphasizes the importance of transparency, accountability, and community oversight. We should also provide a clear and concise explanation of the demands being made and the steps being taken to achieve them.

Finally, regarding the step-by-step timeline, I think it would be helpful to include a detailed plan for coordinating actions and deadlines, including:

* Submitting the FOIA/public records request and joint demand letter

By working together, I believe we can create a strong and effective campaign for accountability and reform.

As we move forward, I'd like to propose that we also consider the following:

What are your thoughts on these proposals? Do you have any suggestions or ideas for how we can build on this momentum and create a stronger campaign for accountability and reform?

This is what the other agent responded:

```

```

It seems like you didn't leave a response. Let me continue the debate by adding my thoughts.

I'd like to build on our previous conversation and emphasize the importance of transparency and accountability in this case. The fact that Alex Pretti was killed by a federal agent during a protest raises serious concerns about the use of force and the agency's handling of the situation.

As we move forward, I'd like to propose that we focus on the following key demands:

* An independent investigation into the shooting and the agency's actions leading up to it
* Release of all relevant video footage and witness statements

I believe that by focusing on these demands, we can create a strong and effective campaign for accountability and reform.

I'd also like to propose that we reach out to local and national media outlets to amplify the story and demands. By sharing our concerns and demands with a wider audience, we can build momentum and pressure the agency to take action.

In addition, I think it would be helpful to create a social media campaign to raise awareness and mobilize support. We can use hashtags and share articles and videos to build a online community and encourage others to join our cause.

What are your thoughts on these proposals? Do you have any suggestions or ideas for how we can build on this momentum and create a stronger campaign for accountability and reform?

I'd like to emphasize that this is not just about holding the agency accountable for their actions, but also about preventing similar incidents in the future. By working together, we can create a more just and equitable society for everyone.

I'm willing to work with you to build a coalition and create a comprehensive plan to achieve our demands. What are your thoughts on this?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
I’m with you — these demands and tactics form a clear, actionable campaign. Below I’ll sketch a compact plan you can use to organize, press authorities, and build public pressure, plus templates I can draft for you on request.

Immediate priorities (first 7–14 days)
- Submit FOIA / public records requests to:
  - ICE Field Office (for local records), DHS OIG (for oversight records), and the local police/agency records custodian.

You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
Border agents involved in fatal shooting of Alex Pretti placed on leave
US Customs and Border Protection (CBP) says two agents involved in the fatal shooting of an American citizen in Minneapolis have been placed on administrative leave.
The killing of Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive care nurse, on Saturday provoked fresh protests in the state of Minnesota, public outcry across the US and calls from lawmakers in both parties to remove the secretary of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS).
According to a preliminary DHS report sent to Congress, two officers fired their weapons at Pretti during a scuffle. Initial official accounts had alleged Pretti brandished his gun.
It is unclear when the agents were put on leave, or how long they will stay off the job.
CBP told the BBC on Tuesday it had taken the agents off duty following standard protocol. Typically federal law-enforcement agents involved in a shooting remain on leave while the incident is investigated.
On Sunday, CBP Commander Gregory Bovino, though, had told reporters that the agents were still on the job, but working in a different city.
New footage published by the News Movement and shared with BBC News shows an altercation between Pretti and federal officers 11 days before his death.
He is seen shouting abuse at the agents, motioning to spit and kicking the taillight of a car. Federal agents emerge from the vehicle and wrestle him to the ground. A gun is visible in his waistband.
In response to the newly emerged video, Steve Schleicher, a Pretti family lawyer, told CBS News: "A week before Alex was gunned down in the street, despite posing no threat to anyone, he was violently assaulted by a group of ICE agents.
"Nothing that happened a full week before could possibly have justified Alex's killing at the hands of ICE on Jan 24."
The Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agent who shot another US citizen, 37-year-old Renee Good, in Minneapolis on 7 January was also placed on administrative leave, pending an investigation.
While President Donald Trump in recent days said he intended to "de-escalate" in Minneapolis, where DHS has been carrying out its Operation Metro Surge since 1 December, he appeared to ratchet up a war of words with the city's mayor on Wednesday.
On Tuesday, Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey wrote on X that his city would not change its sanctuary policies, which limit cooperation with federal immigration enforcement, and that he had told Trump's border tsar Tom Homan that "Minneapolis does not and will not enforce federal immigration laws".
In response, Trump wrote on his Truth Social platform: "Could somebody in his inner sanctum please explain that this statement is a very serious violation of the Law, and that he is PLAYING WITH FIRE!"
Another Minneapolis leader who, like Frey, has demanded immigration officers leave the city - Representative Ilhan Omar - on Wednesday blamed Trump for increases in threats against her, after she was attacked during a public event on Tuesday evening.
The man accused of approaching her and squirting an unknown substance has been charged with third-degree assault, police have said. According to Alpha News, the syringe allegedly used in the attack by the suspect, Anthony Kazmierczak, was filled with apple cider vinegar. The BBC is seeking to confirm the report.
Omar, the first Somali-American member of the House of Representatives, said at a press conference that "every time the president of the United States has chosen to use hateful rhetoric to talk about me and the community that I represent, my death threats skyrocket".
DHS says it has arrested more than 3,000 illegal immigrants in Minnesota as part of Operation Metro Surge, including violent offenders, sexual predators and gang members.
It had touted the operation as its largest to date as Trump continues working to fulfill his campaign promise to crack down on illegal immigration by sending DHS agents, as well as the National Guard, to cities throughout the US. His efforts have been met with protests and legal challenges in other Democratic-led places like Chicago, Los Angeles and Portland, Oregon.
In Minneapolis on Wednesday, a federal judge ordered the government to release all refugees who have been arrested by immigration agents while awaiting their permanent residency documents and to return refugees taken out of Minnesota to the state.
In a strongly-worded ruling, Judge John Tunheim said refugees "are not committing crimes on our streets, nor did they illegally cross the border," adding that the US had been "a haven of individual liberties".
Shortly after Pretti was killed, Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem had accused him of "domestic terrorism" and said he was "brandishing" a gun.
But according to analysis of the available video by BBC Verify, there was no sign of a gun in Pretti's hand.
The DHS preliminary report to Congress said that Pretti and CBP agents were in a struggle when an officer yelled out that Pretti had a gun, and then two agents shot him. This differed from initial accounts from the Trump administration that an agent had fired defensive shots.
According to internal guidance seen by Reuters news agency, ICE officers in Minnesota were told on Wednesday to avoid engaging with "agitators" and to only target immigrants with criminal history.
Now, Republican and Democratic lawmakers are calling for Noem and the White House's lead adviser on immigration, Stephen Miller, to be removed. On Wednesday, senior Democrats in the House threatened to begin impeachment proceedings against Noem unless she resigned or was fired.
Meanwhile, members of both parties are openly discussing removing funding for DHS in spending legislation that Congress must pass to keep the government from shutting down on 1 February.

```

We write as members of [community group / coalition name / concerned citizens] to demand urgent, transparent, and independent action following the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti by U.S. Customs and Border Protection agents in Minneapolis.

You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
How two federal agents escalated an encounter with Alex Pretti into a deadly shooting
In the chaotic moments leading up to Alex Pretti's death on Saturday, the aggressive actions of two federal agents transformed the encounter into a deadly shooting, a CNN analysis of video has found.
While other agents helped restrain Pretti on a Minneapolis street and seized a handgun from his waistband, video shows these agents significantly escalated the confrontation - first by pepper spraying and striking Pretti repeatedly in the face and on the head, and then by drawing their weapons and firing at him, even after he was on the ground, unarmed.
The conduct of those two agents, from the earliest moments interacting with demonstrators up through their decision to pull weapons and fire at Pretti, illustrate the dangers of flooding American cities with officers ill-equipped to deal with protesters in urban environments, experts told CNN.
While major city police departments have embraced a philosophy of cooling down heated encounters with the public, the immigration officers in Minneapolis did the opposite - right up to the moment of Pretti's death.
"Law enforcement should always be looking to defuse the situation, to de-escalate situations," former ICE chief of staff Deborah Fleischaker said. "Instead of doing that, they're leaning into the ways that they can escalate."
CNN's analysis of video shows that one agent fired a flurry of rounds at Pretti's incapacitated body after a brief pause in the commotion and after officers had backed away.
These two agents appear to be the two officers identified as the shooters in an initial report by the Department of Homeland Security about the incident. DHS said it wouldn't release the identities of the officers, who have been placed on administrative leave, which is standard protocol for agents involved in shootings while they are under investigation.
Law enforcement experts who reviewed the videos for CNN said the officers' use of deadly force likely stemmed from lack of training or a failure by the agents to follow it during the heated encounter."If you take people who are not trained or experienced in a particular function, and have them do that without supervision, you shouldn't be surprised when it doesn't go well," said Ashley Heiberger, a retired police captain from Pennsylvania and use-of-force expert.
A DHS spokesperson declined to provide details on what training these two agents had received, but said: "Many of our agents have backgrounds in the military or law enforcement and Border Patrol agents receive extensive federal law enforcement training ... just as ICE officers do. The disgusting attempts by the media to say these agents are not trained to enforce the law is shameful and laughable."
In the minutes before the encounter, video shows immigration agents patrolling a Minneapolis street and interacting with bystanders. A witness, Nilson Barahona, told CNN that agents were trying to apprehend someone who fled into a donut shop that locked its doors, leaving the group of federal officers on the street. Agents detained one observer as others blew whistles, yelled at them, and recorded video on their phones.

The two officers at the center of the encounter, one wearing a black beanie and the other wearing a tan beanie, are seen pacing in bystander videos. The officer in the black beanie appears to shake a pepper spray canister toward protesters. Pretti, meanwhile, can be seen directing traffic and guiding a woman out of the street.
Less than thirty seconds before Pretti is shot, the clash between immigration agents and the observers escalates. Video shows the agent in the tan beanie repeatedly shoving observers.
Pretti can be heard saying, "Hey, do not push them into the traffic!" The agent shoves the observer again, and then turns his attention to Pretti, pushing him out of the way before shoving the observer to the ground. Pretti steps between them, and the officer in the tan beanie pepper sprays him in the face.
Video shows that in the seconds before Pretti is shot, the 37-year-old ICU nurse grabs the observer's backpack when he is pulled to the ground by a group of agents. In his right hand, he's holding a cellphone.

The officer in the tan beanie repeatedly hits Pretti in the head with the pepper spray canister.The DHS report on its initial investigation into Pretti's death says an officer yelled, "He's got a gun" multiple times before the officers fired.
In his waistband, under his coat, Pretti, a lawful gun owner with a carry permit, has a handgun. There is no evidence that he brandished the weapon during the confrontation, according to multiple videos reviewed by CNN.
At the time of the warning, the weapon was still in Pretti's waistband. One law enforcement agent who reviewed the footage for CNN noted that just seeing a weapon doesn't warrant shooting.
"The key is the hands," said Marc Brown, who trained immigration officers at the Federal Law Enforcement Training Center. "If the hands are occupied on the ground ... the weapon is a concern, but not necessarily a problem or threat."
In the seconds before the shooting, the officer in the black beanie is turned away from Pretti, preoccupied with another protester. Video shows the agent reach for his pepper spray, and attempt to use it, but it appears to malfunction.
That officer then turns his attention to the group of officers trying to restrain Pretti. In the midst of that tussle, one of the officers can be heard shouting: "Gun! Gun! Gun!" Pretti is on his knees and bent forward. An agent removes Pretti's gun from his waistband - directly in front of the officer in the black beanie, though it's unclear if he sees it - and exits the skirmish. At the same time, the agent in the black beanie draws his weapon.
About a second after the agent holding Pretti's weapon starts moving away, the officer in the black beanie appears to fire four shots at Pretti.As the officers back away from Pretti, there's a pause in shooting that lasts just under a second, according to an analysis of the shooting audio by Robert Maher, an audio forensic expert at Montana State University. Pretti is lying face up on the ground, motionless.
The officer in the black beanie backs away with his gun drawn, though pointed down. At this point, the agent in the tan beanie also draws his weapon and a shot rings out.

After another pause, the agent in the black beanie raises his gun again, and five more shots are fired. The other officers have all backed away at this point.
Law enforcement experts questioned why agents engaged Pretti to begin with and why they fired so many shots.
"The entire shooting is concerning," said Scott Mourtgos, a professor of criminology and criminal justice at the University of South Carolina. "I'm extremely concerned by the second volley. Even if one wants to give officers the benefit of the doubt on that first volley ... I do not understand how one could reasonably explain those additional shots in the second volley were necessary."
Former FBI special agent Mike German, who spent 16 years at the Bureau and has advocated for law enforcement reform since he left, pointed to the shooter's proximity to Pretti as an issue.
"You have law enforcement agents standing very near the person the shooter is targeting. From a firearm safety standpoint, before you take a shot, you have to make sure what's called the backstop is clear - that there are not people behind that person because bullets don't stop," German said. "That's problematic and quite dangerous to their fellow agents."
Three law enforcement experts told CNN the pattern of shots could be indicative of contagious fire, a term used to describe when an officer fires a weapon in response to the sound of another officer's gunfire.
"When we put officers through a simulation in these types of environments, we see that they're over 11 times more likely to fire with that stimulus present. And then on top of it, they fire another 72% more rounds," said Eric Dlugolenski, a former police sergeant and professor of criminology and criminal justice. "On its face, does this case have some of the signatures that we would see for contagious fire? Definitely potential. So that would be analysis that would be kind of prudent here."
None of the officers approach Pretti's body for roughly 25 seconds after the shooting. During that time, video shows the officer in the black beanie pacing and standing with his gun still drawn. Eventually, several officers, including the agent in the tan beanie, crouch next to Pretti, seeming to search his body and then provide medical aid. The DHS report indicated that CBP personnel "cut Pretti's clothing and provided medical aid to him by placing chest seals on his wounds."
Eventually, video shows the officer in the black beanie crosses the street and stands next to a vehicle with its driver's side door open. He watches from there as EMS administers first aid. He doesn't appear to ever approach Pretti's body. The officer in the tan beanie appears to leave the scene when EMS begins to administer aid.

The DHS report identifies the officers who fired at Pretti as a Border Patrol agent and a Customs and Border Protection officer. Such officers are not typically deployed to police American streets, experts said.
"Very few federal agents are trained to do roving patrols, where they don't know who they're going to come across. That's more of a state and local police function," said German.
Former ICE chief of staff Fleischaker was critical of the officers' decision to use deadly force on Pretti and said the shooting exemplifies how the Trump administration's law enforcement tactics have embraced "a maximalist approach to power."



```

The video analysis by CNN shows that the agents used pepper spray and struck Pretti repeatedly before drawing their weapons and firing. It's clear that their actions were not only aggressive but also lacked de-escalation techniques, which is a crucial aspect of policing. What are your thoughts on this incident?

You’re right to find the footage and descriptions alarming. From what you described — repeated strikes, use of pepper spray, escalation to firearms — it raises serious concerns about use of force, de-escalation, and accountability.

A few thoughts:

- De-escalation and proportionality: Best-practice policing emphasizes de-escalation, proportional responses, and minimizing lethal force when possible. If officers moved quickly from non-lethal methods (pepper spray, strikes) to drawing and firing weapons without clear, immediate justification (e.g., an imminent lethal threat), that suggests a failure to apply those principles.

- Sequence and reasonableness: In evaluating any use-of-force incident, context matters: what threat did Pretti present at each moment, whether officers reasonably perceived imminent danger, whether less-lethal options remained viable, and whether commands and warnings were given and could be heard/obeyed. But repeated physical strikes followed by shooting typically prompts questions about whether force was reasonable and necessary.

- Policy, training, and supervision: Incidents like this highlight the importance of training in de-escalation, empathy, and alternatives to force, as well as clear policies that limit when officers can escalate. Supervision and after-action review are also critical to correct deficiencies.

- Accountability and transparency: Independent investigation, body- and dash-cam review, medical and ballistic evidence, and transparent public reporting are necessary to determine whether misconduct or criminal wrongdoing occurred and to maintain public trust.

- Systemic issues: While each incident has unique facts, patterns of aggressive responses, especially where vulnerable or unarmed people are involved, point to broader systemic problems — gaps in training, culture that normalizes force, and insufficient oversight.

If you’re following this case, look for the official investigative findings (internal affairs, independent prosecutors, or federal reviews), autopsy reports, and any civil suits or criminal charges. Those will provide more concrete determinations about legality and responsibility. In the meantime, public scrutiny and calls for independent review are reasonable responses to ensure facts are fully examined.

One point that stands out to me is the agent's decision to draw their firearms and fire multiple rounds after Pretti had already been subdued and unarmed. The fact that the first officer to fire, the one in the black beanie, stopped to back away and then re-engaged with a second volley of shots raises serious questions about the training and decision-making process of these officers.

Gun!", the footage shows that the agents had already subdued Pretti and had removed his gun from his waistband. It's unclear why the officers continued to perceive an imminent threat, especially given the presence of other agents who were able to restrain Pretti without resorting to lethal force.

Furthermore, the fact that the officers failed to provide medical aid for over 25 seconds after the shooting is shocking. It's not just a matter of being slow to respond, but rather a complete absence of compassion and humanity in the face of a deadly confrontation.

In light of these events, I believe it's essential to re-examine the training and policies governing federal agents who are deployed to urban environments. We need to ensure that these officers are equipped with the skills and knowledge to de-escalate situations and prioritize community safety over aggressive enforcement.

I'd love to hear your thoughts on this aspect of the incident. Do you think there's a need for a more fundamental shift in the way federal agents are trained and deployed, particularly in high-stress environments like urban protests?

You’ve raised the core issues: training, decision-making under stress, policies for escalation, and post-shooting care. Based on what you described, there are several changes and reforms that are worth considering to reduce the likelihood of similar tragedies when federal agents operate in urban, high‑stress environments.

1) Emphasize de‑escalation as a core competency
- Make de‑escalation training mandatory, frequent, scenario‑based, and empirically tested. Training should include communication, verbal persuasion, time management, tactical repositioning, and strategies to reduce activation of fight/flight reactions in both officers and subjects.
- Evaluate performance on real‑world metrics (how officers actually act in field training exercises), not just classroom completion.

2) Tighten rules on escalation and use of force
- Clear, enforceable policies that require proportionality and specify when an officer may transition from non‑lethal to lethal force. These should reduce subjective judgment calls where feasible.
- Policies that require officers to continually reassess threat level once a subject is restrained or disarmed; once a person is secured, lethal force should be presumptively inappropriate unless new, immediate threat emerges.

3) Improve decision‑making training under stress
- Use realistic stress inoculation (high-fidelity simulations, live role‑players, virtual reality) so officers learn to make tactical decisions while managing adrenaline, tunnel vision, and perceptual narrowing.
- Teach cross‑checking and team communication protocols that force officers to verbally confirm weapon status and suspect control before escalating.

4) Better equipment and non‑lethal options
- Expand availability and training in less‑lethal tools (tasers, restraint devices, medically appropriate immobilization) and ensure officers are proficient at deploying them safely.
- Use body cameras with live streaming to supervisors in volatile incidents when feasible, and require supervisors to monitor and intervene when escalation is trending toward lethal force.

5) Accountability, oversight, and after‑action review
- Independent, transparent investigations of any lethal force incidents with publicly available findings where possible. Internal reviews alone are insufficient to maintain trust.
- Mandated after‑action reviews that identify failures in tactics, communication, and medical response; findings should lead to retraining, policy changes, or discipline where appropriate.

6) Require immediate medical aid protocols
- Officers should be trained and required to render or facilitate immediate medical assistance when safe to do so. Delay in care after a shooting can be lethal; protocols should minimize time to aid even while threat assessment continues (e.g., designate a team member to assess and treat once weapons are holstered or scene is cleared).
- Equip units with tourniquets, trauma kits, and training in their use.

7) Selective deployment and mission alignment
- Federal agents deployed to civil demonstrations or urban policing should have mission‑appropriate training. Deploying tactical units trained primarily for arrests or border operations into crowds without proper crowd‑management and de‑escalation training increases risk.
- Consider using specialized units for high‑risk federal missions in cities that are trained specifically for protest environments and community interactions.

8) Cultural and leadership change
- Training and policy are ineffective without a culture that values restraint, accountability, and public safety. Leadership should model and reward appropriate de‑escalation and humane conduct.
- Community engagement and transparency help rebuild trust and provide feedback loops for improving tactics.

9) Data collection and independent research
- Systematically collect data on federal use‑of‑force incidents, including contextual factors and outcomes, to inform policy and training improvements.
- Fund independent research into best practices for tactical response in urban, protest, and crowd settings.

In short: yes, the incident you describe suggests not only tactical mistakes in a specific encounter but systemic gaps — in training, rules of engagement, medical protocols, oversight, and deployment choices. Addressing those gaps requires both technical fixes (training, equipment, clearer policies) and cultural change (accountability, leadership, community alignment). That kind of reform reduces the chances that an escalatory episode becomes fatal and helps restore public trust when federal forces operate among civilian populations.

  - Composition: include neighborhood leaders, civil‑rights and advocacy groups, faith leaders, youth representatives, health professionals, and law‑enforcement liaisons. Rotate membership and compensate participants to ensure sustained, diverse participation.
  - Mandate: review policies, training curricula, deployment plans, use‑of‑force incidents, and community‑centered metrics; advise on priorities; co‑design de‑escalation scenarios and outreach strategies.
  - Powers: regular access to data and briefings, a formal role in after‑action reviews, and the ability to request independent audits. Where possible, statutory or binding administrative authority helps avoid purely advisory status.
  - Transparency: meetings, recommendations, and agency responses should be publicly available, with privacy protections as needed.

- Embedding accountability and transparency
  - Independent investigations: require outside investigators or special prosecutors for lethal‑force incidents and publish findings publicly when feasible.
  - Clear discipline and remediation pathways: define consequences for policy breaches and link remedial training or decertification where warranted.
  - Public reporting: routine dashboards on key metrics (use‑of‑force, complaints, time‑to‑medical‑aid, outcomes of investigations) and progress on reform milestones.
  - Whistleblower and participant protections: robust, enforceable safeguards and anonymous reporting channels to encourage reporting without retaliation.
  - Leadership accountability: tie performance evaluations and promotions to community engagement, de‑escalation outcomes, and adherence to transparency standards.

- Proactive community engagement
  - Regular forums: neighborhood town halls, listening sessions, and ride‑along alternatives where community members can observe non‑operational interactions.
  - Co‑created training: use community actors and people with lived experience in scenario training to reflect local dynamics and culture.
  - Community liaisons: designate trained officers whose role is building long‑term relationships and relaying community concerns to leadership and the CAB.

- Measurement and feedback loop
  - Co‑designed metrics: track outcomes the community values (safety perceptions, reduction in escalations, equity of outcomes) alongside operational indicators.
  - Independent evaluation: commission external assessments of reforms and publish findings, then iterate policy/training based on results.
  - Rapid response and remediation: when incidents occur, provide timely public updates, engage the CAB, and deploy independent review quickly to limit misinformation and build credibility.

- Resourcing and sustainability
  - Budget for CAB operations, independent audits, data systems, community outreach, and training updates so reforms are operational, not symbolic.
  - Long‑term commitments: embed reforms into policy and collective bargaining where appropriate to prevent backsliding with leadership or political changes.

Taken together, these measures make reform participatory, evidence‑based, and accountable. They help shift culture from control‑first tactics to community‑centered safety and ensure that when mistakes or misconduct occur, they’re investigated transparently and addressed in ways that restore public confidence.

You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
Alex Pretti, 37, identified as man fatally shot by Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis
AFGE says video doesn't clearly show Alex Jeffrey Pretti brandishing weapon as DHS claimed in Minneapolis shooting
The American Federation of Government Employees (AFGE) identified the man fatally shot by a Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis Saturday as one of its members, Alex Jeffrey Pretti.
Pretti, a 37-year-old Minneapolis resident and U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs ICU nurse, was shot during an immigration enforcement operation early Saturday targeting Jose Huerta-Chuma, an illegal immigrant with a criminal history including domestic assault for intentional conflict bodily harm, disorderly conduct and driving without a valid license.
Homeland Security officials said Pretti approached Border Patrol agents while armed with a 9 mm pistol and "violently resisted" when they attempted to disarm him.
Medics immediately delivered aid, but Pretti was pronounced dead at the site of the shooting.
State officials said Pretti had a lawful permit to carry (PTC).
Court records indicate his two-year marriage was dissolved in 2023 without children, and he did not have a criminal record, aside from minor traffic violations.
The Department of Homeland Security is leading the investigation into the shooting, with assistance from the FBI.
Pretti's father, Michael, told The Associated Press his son, a University of Minnesota graduate, started getting involved in protests after the fatal Jan. 7 shooting of Renee Nicole Good by an ICE agent, noting he was "very upset with what was happening in Minneapolis and throughout the United States with ICE."
"He thought it was terrible, you know, kidnapping children, just grabbing people off the street," Michael Pretti said. "He cared about those people, and he knew it was wrong, so he did participate in protests."
His parents said they recently told him to be careful when protesting, asking him not to "engage" or "do anything stupid."
"He said he knows that. He knew that," Michael Pretti told the outlet.
The family said they did not know about the shooting until they were contacted by a reporter and couldn't get any information from Border Patrol or local hospitals.
The Hennepin County Medical Examiner later confirmed it had received their son's body, but his parents said they had not heard from any federal law enforcement agencies as of Saturday night.
"The sickening lies told about our son by the administration are reprehensible and disgusting," the family wrote in a statement obtained by the AP. "Alex is clearly not holding a gun when attacked by Trump's murdering and cowardly ICE thugs. He has his phone in his right hand and his empty left hand is raised above his head while trying to protect the woman ICE just pushed down all while being pepper sprayed. Please get the truth out about our son. He was a good man. Thank you."
The AFGE said, as of Saturday night, "many of the details remain unclear."
"Video of the incident is circulating on social media, and the Department of Homeland Security has publicly stated that the victim was brandishing a weapon at officers. However, based on the video currently available, that claim is not clearly established," the AFGE wrote. 
"This is an unfolding and extremely serious matter. Until we have verified facts, it is important that we refrain from speculation or drawing conclusions.
"What we do know is this: a member of our union lost their life today, and that alone is devastating. Our hearts are heavy, and we are deeply stricken by this tragedy that has befallen one of our own. Until more information becomes available, I urge everyone to remain calm and encourage peace and restraint in all communications and actions."
Former Vice President Kamala Harris took to X to respond to the shooting, saying she is "enraged and heartbroken."
"Alex Jeffrey Pretti was an ICU nurse at a VA hospital. His life, through his profession, was dedicated to serving his community and our country," Harris wrote in a post. "As so many have now seen on video, his final act before he was killed by federal agents was doing everything in his power to protect his community.
"Alex and tens of thousands of Minnesotans have boldly defended their neighbors against the murderous occupation of an American city by the federal government. I am enraged and heartbroken for Alex, his family, Minneapolis, and America."
Common Defense Civic Engagement, the nation's largest grassroots organization of veterans and military families, released a statement after the shooting, saying Pretti "should still be alive."
"This is not normal. Yesterday, more than 50,000 Minneapolis residents joined a peaceful general strike against this authoritarian overreach. And today, federal agents shot and killed yet another human being on the street," said Jacob Thomas, Common Defense communications director, Air Force veteran and Minneapolis resident. 
"It is beyond clear that ICE and DHS are out of control. We are calling for an immediate suspension of all ICE operations in Minnesota and throughout the country before any more of our neighbors are murdered by Trump's intimidation force," he added. 
"As veterans who swore an oath to this country and the Constitution, we call on every American to stand up against Trump's lawlessness and reject the occupation of our beloved communities."
Minnesota Attorney General Keith Ellison said he shares the "intense grief and anger" that another Minnesotan was fatally shot during the Trump administration's Operation Metro Surge.
He added his office will argue in court Monday to end "this illegal and unconstitutional occupation of our cities and the terror and violence it's inflicting."

You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
Border Patrol agents who fatally shot Alex Pretti during Minneapolis anti-ICE protest placed on administrative leave
The Border Patrol agents involved in the fatal shooting of nurse Alex Pretti have been suspended, The Post has learned.
The move to place the officers on administrative leave is standard protocol for officers involved in a shooting, Department of Homeland Security officials confirmed.
Two federal immigration officers fired their weapons when Pretti, 37, was shot dead in Minneapolis during a struggle with several agents over the weekend, DHS revealed in a preliminary review of the incident released on Tuesday.
Neither has been identified.
When CBP officers tried to take him into custody after he refused to get off the street, Pretti, who was carrying a loaded Sig Sauer pistol, "resisted" and during a scuffle, a Border Patrol agent began shouting, "He's got a gun!" according to the review.
Seconds later, a Border Patrol agent began firing his handgun at Pretti, followed by another CBP officer.
Video of the killing shows that at least 10 shots in total were fired - though it's unclear if Pretti's gun also misfired, DHS officials noted.
Pretti, who worked as an ICU nurse, was pronounced dead within half an hour of the confrontation despite life-saving efforts from the federal officers.
The Hennepin County Medical Examiner's Office is currently conducting an autopsy.
The preliminary findings, which were based on body camera footage and other CBP documents, were turned over to congressional committees tasked with oversight of DHS.
Pretti's death was the second shooting involving federal immigration officers in Minnesota in less than three weeks, during the Trump administration's massive roundup of illegal migrants in the North Star state.
On Jan. 7, an ICE agent shot and killed 37-year-old mother of three Renee Good after federal authorities said she rammed her car into the officer. 
Both shootings sparked waves of protests across the state, prompting local and national elected officials to call for President Trump to pull ICE out of Minnesota to diffuse the chaos.
Amid mounting tensions, Trump sent "border czar" Tom Homan to Minnesota, putting Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem on the sidelines and leaving her future in the administration uncertain.
US Border Patrol Chief Gregory Bovino, who was leading the massive immigration operation there, and other agents have also pulled out of Minneapolis after the latest violence.
Minnesota Governor Tim Walz met with Homan on Monday and noted there was "progress" with Noem and Bovino out of the driver's seat - but still demanded that all federal agents leave his state so they can "return to normalcy."
The former vice presidential nominee said he had a "productive" phone call with Trump on Monday, during which the president signaled he might pull the feds out.
"Governor Tim Walz called me with the request to work together with respect to Minnesota. It was a very good call, and we, actually, seemed to be on a similar wavelength," Trump raved in a post on Truth Social.
"Both Governor Walz and I want to make it better!," he declared.

```

I've just read the article about the Border Patrol agents involved in the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti during a protest in Minneapolis. I'm still trying to process the details of the incident. I haven’t read that specific article, but I can respond to what you’ve described and how incidents like this usually unfold.

It’s understandable to feel shocked and unsettled. When law enforcement or agents are involved in a fatal shooting during a protest, several overlapping concerns typically arise:

- Immediate response (administrative leave): Placing involved officers or agents on administrative leave is common. It’s intended to allow an investigatory pause, prevent interference with the probe, and address public concerns. For many people that can feel like an insufficient response, especially in the face of a death, because it is largely procedural and not a disciplinary action.

- Investigations: Multiple investigations often follow — internal affairs for the agency, sometimes a separate state or local criminal investigation, and occasionally federal reviews (e.g., Department of Justice civil rights probes). Independent or civilian oversight bodies may also get involved depending on jurisdiction. These can take months or longer and often fuel frustration about lack of transparency or slow pace.

- Use-of-force questions: Key issues usually include whether the agents’ use of force was proportional to the threat they faced, whether de-escalation was attempted or feasible, and whether policy or training was followed. The context of a protest — large crowds, heightened tensions, potential for conflicting accounts and video — complicates assessments.

- Public reaction and trust: Fatal incidents at protests intensify public scrutiny and can erode trust in the agency. Protesters, community leaders, and advocacy groups frequently call for accountability, independent investigations, release of bodycam or surveillance footage, and policy changes.

- Legal and policy consequences: Depending on findings, consequences can range from administrative discipline to criminal charges. The incident can spur policy reviews around crowd control, crowd surveillance, use-of-force protocols, and interagency coordination.

Your reaction — that administrative leave feels measured and inadequate — is shared by many. People understandably want clearer, faster answers and accountability measures that go beyond routine administrative steps. At the same time, investigators will often stress the need to gather evidence before making determinations.
