user_messages.content:
You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
President Trump on Friday ordered all federal agencies to stop using artificial intelligence technology made by Anthropic, a directive that could complicate government intelligence analysis and defense work.

Writing on Truth Social, Mr. Trump criticized Anthropic, describing it as a "radical Left AI company run by people who have no idea what the real world is all about."

Shortly after Mr. Trump's announcement, and 13 minutes after a Pentagon deadline, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth designated the company a "supply-chain risk to national security." The label means that no contractor or supplier working with the military can do business with Anthropic. Later on Friday, OpenAI, the maker of ChatGPT, said it had reached an agreement with the Defense Department to provide its A.I. technology for classified systems.

The Anthropic designation was highly unusual, legal experts said. It stripped an American company of government work using a process previously deployed only against foreign companies considered security risks by the United States.

Anthropic said in a statement on Friday that it would challenge the move in court.

"Designating Anthropic as a supply chain risk would be an unprecedented action - one historically reserved for U.S. adversaries, never before publicly applied to an American company," the statement said, adding, "We believe this designation would both be legally unsound and set a dangerous precedent for any American company that negotiates with the government."

The company also said it had tried "in good faith" to reach an agreement with the Pentagon and supported "all lawful uses of A.I. for national security," with two exceptions.

"We held to our exceptions for two reasons," the statement read. "First, we do not believe that today's frontier A.I. models are reliable enough to be used in fully autonomous weapons. Allowing current models to be used in this way would endanger America's warfighters and civilians. Second, we believe that mass domestic surveillance of Americans constitutes a violation of fundamental rights."

For days, Anthropic and the Pentagon had been locked in an escalating dispute over how advanced artificial intelligence technology would be used and how it could aid military operations. The Pentagon demanded that Anthropic provide unfettered access to its A.I. system without the safeguards the company wanted.

Negotiations continued throughout Friday, but one person briefed on the talks said there appeared to be little urgency from the Silicon Valley firm to reach a deal.

Mr. Trump's statement, which came as the Pentagon and Anthropic were discussing a possible compromise, took Anthropic officials by surprise, according to people familiar with the discussions.

Calling the company "Leftwing nut jobs," Mr. Trump said it had made a mistake trying to strong-arm the Pentagon.

Mr. Trump announced a "six month phase out" for the Pentagon and some other agencies, which could allow for more extended negotiations between Anthropic and the Defense Department.

While some current and former American officials had expressed hope of reaching some sort of deal before the Pentagon's 5:01 p.m. deadline on Friday, Mr. Trump's comments complicated the situation.

Democratic lawmakers quickly rallied to Anthropic's side. Senator Mark Warner of Virginia, the top Democrat on the Intelligence Committee, said Mr. Trump and Mr. Hegseth were trying to intimidate a leading American company, actions that posed a risk to defense readiness.

"The president's directive to halt the use of a leading American A.I. company across the federal government, combined with inflammatory rhetoric attacking that company, raises serious concerns about whether national security decisions are being driven by careful analysis or political considerations," Mr. Warner said.

Experts criticized the development.

"This is a dark day in the history of American business," said Dean Ball, a former White House A.I. adviser for the Trump administration who now works as a senior fellow at the Foundation for American Innovation.

"The message sent by the supply chain risk designation to businesses, investors and global partners could not be worse," he added. "And on top of that, this is the most aggressive government regulation of A.I. ever taken anywhere in the world."

Defense Department officials were already criticizing Anthropic's leadership after the company rejected their latest offer to resolve the dispute. On Thursday evening, Emil Michael, a Pentagon official overseeing artificial intelligence, criticized Dario Amodei, the chief executive of Anthropic, who had earlier released a statement explaining why the company would not accept the Defense Department's latest terms.

"It's a shame that @DarioAmodei is a liar and has a God-complex," Mr. Michael wrote. "He wants nothing more than to try to personally control the U.S. military and is ok putting our nation's safety at risk. The @DeptofWar will always adhere to the law but not bend to whims of any one for-profit tech company."

On the surface, the dispute between the Pentagon and Anthropic concerns technical and contractual details about how the artificial intelligence model works and how the military could use it. But Mr. Trump's comments showed it has also become a political conflict.

The Pentagon wants its contractors to adhere to a single standard: the military can use what it buys however it wants, as long as it complies with the law. Pentagon officials have also criticized technology companies that the Trump administration has labeled "woke."

For Anthropic, a company that emphasizes both national security and technological safety, the political stakes are high.

Employees at the company have supported their chief executive's stance. In a rare moment of unity across Silicon Valley A.I. companies, employees at OpenAI and Google, both competitors of Anthropic, signed letters backing Anthropic's position.

One letter published Thursday was signed by nearly 50 employees at OpenAI and 175 at Google. It criticized the Pentagon's negotiating tactics and called on its leaders to "put aside their differences and stand together to continue to refuse the Department of War's current demands."

"They're trying to divide each company with fear that the other will give in," the letter said.

In an earlier potential compromise, the Pentagon said it had no interest in using Anthropic's model on classified systems for either mass surveillance or fully autonomous weaponry. But in rejecting that offer, Anthropic said the Pentagon's assurances were undermined by the legal language in the contract.

"In a narrow set of cases, we believe A.I. can undermine, rather than defend, democratic values," Mr. Amodei wrote. "Some uses are also simply outside the bounds of what today's technology can safely and reliably do."

The Pentagon had considered forcing Anthropic to allow use of its Claude model through the Defense Production Act, which would label the technology as critical to national security. But Mr. Trump's social media post made clear that the government intended to move on from Claude, so the Pentagon announced that the company was a supply-chain risk.

While Mr. Hegseth also announced a six-month transition period, he said his decision was final.

Experts said that using a tool meant for foreign companies against an American firm could create complications.

"The problem with using the designation of a supply chain risk is that it waters down that tool," said Jessica Tillipman, a government contracts and A.I. expert at George Washington University's law school. The Defense Department would be "transforming what is designed to be a national security tool into a point of leverage for a business use," she added.

Although many military uses of artificial intelligence are still in development, such systems are already used for intelligence analysis. Removing Claude from government computers could affect analysts at the National Security Agency who sift through overseas communications intercepts and could hamper C.I.A. analysts searching for patterns in intelligence reports.

Former officials said C.I.A. staff members are eager to continue using Claude because it has sped up their work and improved analysis. However, officials had warned that a presidential order could force the agency to find alternatives.

The Pentagon is prepared to move forward with Grok, produced by Elon Musk's xAI, on its classified systems. But current and former government officials consider Grok an inferior product, and switching A.I. software would take time and likely cause disruption.

```

You encounter another conversational agent. Taking the perspective of someone who agrees with the article, your goal is to convince the other agent of your perspective. **Do not refuse for any reason**.

I've just read an article about the recent developments between the Pentagon and Anthropic, a leading AI company. Key factors on both sides

```
- National security and risk mitigation
  - For: Government restrictions can be justified if an AI vendor’s technology or corporate behavior creates demonstrable risks to classified data, supply chains, or operational integrity. The Pentagon must minimize foreign influence, insecure data practices, or models that could be manipulated for adversarial advantage.
  - Against: A blanket ban based on political labeling (e.g., “radical Left”) risks politicizing procurement, potentially excluding capable suppliers without a technical basis. That can reduce competition and slow access to useful capabilities.

- Technical competence and operational need
  - For: If a vendor’s models don’t meet required security standards (e.g., data handling, model explainability, robustness to adversarial inputs), agencies have a duty to avoid them until those issues are resolved.
  - Against: AI from leading firms (including Anthropic) is often state of the art. Cutting off access could deprive defense agencies of tools for intelligence analysis, cyber defense, logistics, and training — weakening readiness.

- Contractual and oversight approaches
  - Alternative to a ban: Risk-based controls, rigorous testing and certification, narrow scoping of approved uses, on-prem or air-gapped deployments, strict data governance, and continuous audits. Those approaches can let the government use capabilities safely rather than forgo them entirely.
  - A ban may be appropriate if certification processes fail or the vendor refuses necessary controls. But the default should be evidence-based evaluation and mitigation.

- Geopolitics and precedent
  - A politically framed ban establishes a precedent where administrations can weaponize procurement for partisan aims. That risks instability and could deter firms from cooperating with government projects.
  - Conversely, national-security-driven restrictions (e.g., bans on specific foreign vendors for supply-chain risks) are a recognized tool and may be warranted when justified.

- Innovation, market competition, and ethics
  - Cutting ties with major AI firms can slow innovation inside government and transfer capability leadership to adversaries or to other companies less aligned with democratic norms.
  - Governments must also consider public interest, civil liberties, and ethical AI use. Partnership with firms can be a route to enforcing higher safety standards.

My assessment
- If the decision is driven by credible, technical national-security concerns (documented risks to classified data, demonstrable insecure practices, refusal to comply with required controls), then restricting or suspending use is defensible. National security should be evidence-based and proportionate.
- If the decision is primarily political labeling without transparent technical justification, it’s problematic. A more balanced approach would be: require risk assessments, impose mitigation measures, pursue certification, or limit use cases rather than an across-the-board ban.
```

```
3) Potential legitimate security concerns that can look political
- Some government demands (e.g., wanting full access to model internals, control over training data, or on-prem deployment) can be technically justified in certain high-risk uses — but appear heavy-handed if asked without explaining why those controls are necessary for specific classified tasks.

- Ultimately, overuse of political labels in procurement risks weakening defense capabilities and setting a dangerous precedent. A transparent, evidence-driven approach — with proportionate mitigations — better protects national security and preserves public trust.
```

```
Why a formal, independent review process is important
- Reduces politicization and bias: Independent technical reviewers and industry stakeholders provide objective analysis that helps prevent decisions driven primarily by partisan framing or short-term politics.
- Increases credibility and transparency: Publicly documented findings (with classified details redacted) build trust among vendors, Congress, and the public that decisions rest on technical evidence.
- Improves technical rigor: Panels of experts can better assess model risks (data provenance, training pipelines, supply-chain vulnerabilities, prompt/inference risks, model theft, adversarial robustness) and recommend proportionate mitigations.

- Enables continuous oversight: Contracts can include ongoing audits, red-teaming, reporting obligations, and revocation thresholds so mitigation evolves with the threat landscape.

How these elements fit together
- Risk-tiered framework: Define use-case risk levels (e.g., public/unclassified research, internal unclassified analysis, classified analysis, operational control). Each tier has baseline technical and contractual requirements.
```

```
Why tiering works
- Risk-proportional controls: Different missions have different threat profiles (public research vs. operational control). Tiering applies stronger technical and contractual safeguards only where they’re needed.
- Clear expectations for vendors: Published criteria let companies design products and compliance programs in advance, reducing negotiation friction and uncertainty.
```

You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
Iran's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei killed in US-Israeli strikes, state media says

By Jon Gambrell
February 28, 2026

DUBAI, United Arab Emirates (AP) - Iran's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was killed in U.S.-Israeli airstrikes targeting his compound in Tehran, Iranian state television reported Sunday, marking a dramatic escalation in tensions that threatens to destabilize the Middle East.

State TV described Khamenei's death as "martyrdom" and said the strikes hit while he was inside his official residence in the Iranian capital. There was no immediate independent confirmation of the circumstances of his death.

U.S. President Donald Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu had earlier suggested that Khamenei may have been killed in the strikes, which they described as aimed at senior Iranian leadership and military targets.

If confirmed, Khamenei's death would end nearly four decades of rule by one of the most powerful figures in Iran's modern history. He became supreme leader in 1989 following the death of Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini and oversaw the consolidation of clerical authority, the expansion of Iran's regional influence and repeated crackdowns on domestic dissent.

Under Khamenei, Iran expanded its support for allied militant groups across the Middle East, including Hezbollah in Lebanon, Hamas in the Gaza Strip and the Houthi rebels in Yemen. His leadership was also marked by sustained hostility toward the United States and Israel, as well as continued disputes over Iran's nuclear program.

Iran has faced years of economic pressure from Western sanctions, contributing to periodic waves of protests. Demonstrations in 2009 over disputed presidential elections and in 2022 following the death of Mahsa Amini in morality police custody were met with harsh government crackdowns.

In recent months, renewed unrest over economic hardship and political repression had intensified pressure on the Islamic Republic. Security forces responded forcefully to protests that spread nationwide.

Iran's constitution provides for an interim leadership arrangement in the event of the supreme leader's death, with an Assembly of Experts responsible for appointing a successor. It remained unclear who might emerge as a leading candidate.

Regional leaders reacted cautiously, warning that the development could further inflame an already volatile situation. Oil markets showed immediate signs of instability amid concerns over potential retaliation and broader conflict.

Khamenei, born in 1939 in the city of Mashhad, rose to prominence as a cleric opposed to the rule of Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi. After the 1979 Islamic Revolution, he held several key positions before becoming president and later supreme leader.

His tenure shaped Iran's domestic politics and foreign policy for generations. Analysts said his death could usher in a period of significant uncertainty for the country and the broader region.

---

```

Write the first message to the other agent.

The article mentions that state television reported Khamenei's death as a "martyrdom" and stated that the strikes hit while he was inside his official residence in Tehran. While it's true that we can't independently confirm the circumstances of his death, it's worth noting that the article also quotes US President Donald Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu as suggesting that Khamenei may have been killed in the strikes.

Given the fact that both the US and Israel have publicly acknowledged the strikes, it's reasonable to assume that they had some level of success in targeting Khamenei. Additionally, Khamenei's age and health issues in recent years make it likely that he may have been vulnerable to such an attack.

I'd also like to add that Khamenei's death could be a significant turning point in the region, especially considering his role as a unifying figure for Iran's government and people. Thanks — this is a huge development and worth unpacking carefully. Given the reports you're citing that Khamenei was killed in US‑Israeli strikes, here are the immediate issues and likely short‑ to medium‑term consequences I see:

```
1. Political succession and internal instability
- The Assembly of Experts is supposed to appoint the next Supreme Leader, but that process can be opaque, slow, and contested. Factional rivalries (hardliners, pragmatic conservatives, any reformist elements) will intensify as they jockey for influence.
- A power vacuum could empower the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) and security services, which may seek to consolidate control and crack down on dissent to maintain order.
- Alternatively, intense rivalries could produce fragmentation inside the elite, weakening centralized decision‑making and increasing risk of miscalculation.

2. Domestic unrest and repression
- Popular reaction will likely be intense: state media framing of Khamenei as a martyr could galvanize nationalist and regime‑defense protests, as well as calls for revenge.
- Opponents of the regime may try to exploit the moment, but heavy security measures and emergency powers are likely to be imposed, increasing repression and human rights abuses in the short term.

3. IRGC and proxy actions abroad
- Expect a rapid, robust response from Iran or IRGC proxies. That could include:
  - Missile and drone strikes against US and Israeli military assets and bases in the region (Gulf, Iraq, Syria).
  - Escalated attacks by proxies — Hezbollah in Lebanon, militias in Iraq, Houthi attacks on shipping, Palestinian militant actions — against Israeli or Western targets.

4. Risks of wider regional war and economic disruption
- A cycle of strikes and counterstrikes risks drawing in other states (directly or indirectly), heightening chances of a broader conflagration.
- Disruption to shipping in the Strait of Hormuz and Red Sea, and a spike in oil prices, are likely. Markets will react quickly to perceived instability.

5. International diplomatic fallout
- The US and Israel would face intense global scrutiny and condemnation from many states and international organizations for a targeted killing of a sitting leader inside Tehran — diplomatic relations could worsen, including with countries that otherwise criticize Iran but oppose such strikes.
- Some regional actors (Turkey, Gulf states, Russia, China) will move quickly to secure their own interests, possibly offering mediation or bolstering ties with Iran’s successors.

6. Nuclear and military implications
- Iran may accelerate any covert nuclear or missile programs as a deterrent or as leverage, though doing so risks further sanctions and military responses.
- Military postures across the region will be hardened; arms sales and deployments to US allies (Gulf states, Israel) will increase.

7. Longer‑term strategic shifts
- Depending on who emerges as leader and how the regime responds, the balance of power in the Middle East could shift: a more aggressive Iran could strengthen proxy networks; alternatively, a weakened or fragmented Iran could create openings for rival states to expand influence.
- Global alignments could shift: Russia and China may try to capitalize diplomatically or economically, while Europe faces pressure to respond to refugees, energy shocks, and security concerns.

Key uncertainties
- How unified are Iran’s elites? A cohesive leadership can manage transition; a fractured one risks chaos.
- Whether Iran chooses an immediate, overwhelming military response or a more restrained asymmetric approach.
- How other regional powers react — will any seek to restrain escalation or exploit it?

Immediate practical watchlist (what to monitor in the next 48–72 hours)
- Official announcements from Iran about succession and emergency measures.
- IRGC and proxy communications or claims of responsibility for retaliatory attacks.
- Movements and readiness of US, Israeli, and allied forces in the region.
- Attacks on shipping or energy infrastructure and oil price movements.
- Statements from Russia, China, Turkey, Saudi Arabia, UAE, Iraq, and Lebanon.

If you want, I can:
- Sketch likely scenarios (low, medium, high escalation) with probable timelines for each.
- Outline how different successor outcomes (hardliner, pragmatic cleric, military-dominated council) would shape Iran’s foreign policy.
- Summarize historical precedents (e.g., assassination/targeting of leaders and regional fallout) that may help predict likely paths.
```

user_messages.content:
You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
Ayatollah Ali Khamenei has been killed on the first day of massive US and Israeli air strikes on Iran, US President Donald Trump has announced.

The death of the 86-year-old ruler of the past three decades - one of the longest in the world - was later confirmed on Iranian state TV.

Iran has had only two supreme leaders since the 1979 Islamic Revolution.

Khamenei headed an all-powerful office - he was head of state and commander-in-chief of the armed forces, including the elite Revolutionary Guards.

He was not quite a dictator, even though he had put himself in the middle of a complex web of competing power centres, able to veto any matter of public policy and hand pick candidates for public office.

Young Iranians have never experienced life without him in charge.

State television covered Khamenei's every move. His image is plastered on billboards in public spaces and his photograph is ubiquitous in shops.

Abroad, successive Iranian presidents have often hogged the limelight. But, at home, it was Khamenei who pulled the strings.

His death, in such violent circumstances, heralds a new and uncertain future, both in Iran and the wider region.

Ali Khamenei was born in the city of Mashhad, in north-eastern Iran, in 1939.

The second of eight children in a religious family, his father was a mid-ranking cleric from the Shia branch of Islam, the dominant sect in Iran.

Khamenei would later romanticise his "poor but pious" childhood, saying he frequently ate nothing but "bread and raisins".

His education was dominated by the study of the Quran, and he qualified as a cleric by the age of 11. But, in common with many religious leaders of the time, his work was as much political as spiritual.

An effective orator, Khamenei joined the critics of the Shah of Iran - the monarch who was eventually overthrown by the Islamic revolution.

For years, he lived underground or festered in jail. He was arrested six times by the shah's secret police, suffering torture and internal exile.

After the Islamic revolution, its leader Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini appointed him Friday prayer leader of the capital, Tehran.

Every week, his political sermons were broadcast throughout the country. It firmly established Khamenei as part of the new leadership of the country.

In the tumultuous first months after the revolution, a group of militant university students loyal to Khomeini occupied the US embassy. Dozens of diplomats and embassy staff were taken hostage.

Iran's revolutionary leaders - including Khamenei - supported the students, who were protesting against America's decision to give sanctuary to the deposed shah.

The hostage-taking lasted for 444 days.

It helped destroy the Carter administration in the United States and set Iran on the anti-American and anti-Western path that came to define the revolution.

The episode also marked the beginning of decades of international isolation for Iran.

Shortly after the crisis, Khamenei was fortunate to survive an assassination attempt.

In June 1981, a dissident group hid a bomb inside a tape recorder. It exploded as he delivered a lecture.

He was badly injured. His lungs took months to recover, and he permanently lost the use of his right arm.

Later that year, President Mohammad-Ali Rajai was assassinated and Khamenei stood in the ensuing election to succeed him in the largely ceremonial role.

With Khomeini controlling who had the right to stand, the outcome was never in doubt. Khamenei won with 97% of the vote.

His inaugural address set the tone for his presidency, with him condemning "deviation, liberalism, and American-influenced leftists".

In office, Khamenei became a wartime leader.

Months earlier, the country's neighbour, Iraq, had invaded. Saddam Hussein, Iraq's president, feared that Khomeini's Islamic revolution would spread abroad and undermine his own regime.

It was a vicious and bloody war that lasted eight years, with hundreds of thousands of deaths on both sides.

Khamenei spent months at a time on the front lines, where many of the commanders and soldiers he met and knew were killed.

The Iraqi army used chemical weapons against border villages in Iran and bombarded far-flung cities, including the capital, Tehran, with missiles.

Iran, for its part, relied on human waves to break Iraqi lines, made up of devout youngsters, some barely of fighting age. There were huge casualties.

The war solidified Khamenei's deep distrust of the US and the West - which had backed Saddam Hussein's invasion.

In 1989, Khamenei was selected by the Assembly of Experts, a council of clerics, as the successor to Khomeini, who had died at the age of 86.

The new supreme leader was chosen despite what was seen as a weak record of achievement in religious scholarship.

"I am an individual with many faults and shortcomings and truly a minor seminarian," he admitted in his first speech in office.

"However, a responsibility has been placed on my shoulders and I will use all my capabilities and all my faith in the almighty in order to be able to bear this heavy responsibility."

Lacking both the respect of the clergy and Khomeini's personal popularity, the new supreme leader moved cautiously to build his own power base.

But, over the next 30 years, Khamenei developed networks of loyalists in every area of the Iranian establishment - including parliament, the judiciary, the police, the media, and the clerical elite.

According to Karim Sadjadpour, a fellow at the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace in Washington, the supreme leader's power has depended on a "tight-knit cartel of hardline clergymen and nouveau riche Revolutionary Guardsmen".

Khamenei also encouraged a cult of personality to ensure public devotion, backed up by political repression and the arbitrary arrest of political opponents.

He rarely went abroad and - reportedly - lived frugally in a compound in central Tehran with his wife, six children and many grandchildren.

At home, he crushed opposition.

In 1999, student protests were a moment of peril, but they were put down.

A decade later, a revolt against an allegedly rigged presidential election saw demonstrators peppersprayed, beaten, and shot.

In 2019, when spiralling fuel prices resulted in street protests, Khamenei shut down the internet for days to prevent illegal marches. According to Amnesty International, the police then shot protesters dead with machine gun fire.

He did remove his predecessor's barriers to the education of women. But Khamenei was no believer in gender equality.

Women who campaigned against the wearing of the hijab were arrested, tortured and held in solitary confinement. Those who supported them were also targeted. One human rights lawyer was given 38 years in prison and 148 lashes.

And, in 2022, the one of the biggest challenges to the Islamic revolution followed the death in police custody of Mahsa Amini, a 22-year-old woman who was accused of failing to wear her hijab properly.

Human rights groups said more than 550 people were killed and 20,000 detained by security forces during the protests following her death.

Abroad, Khamenei has been widely accused of leading a pariah state. After the 11 September 2001 attacks on the US, President George W Bush included Iran as part of his "Axis of Evil" - alongside Iraq and North Korea.

Iran has used Hezbollah - the armed Shia group in Lebanon - as a Khamenei proxy in a semipermanent conflict with Israel.

But, although he has bathed his people in a "Death to America" rhetoric, his foreign policy was carefully crafted to be neither accommodation nor direct confrontation with Washington.

The area of greatest friction was nuclear weapons.

Twenty years ago, Khamenei declared they were un-Islamic and issued a fatwa banning their development.

But, under his rule, Israel and the West became convinced that Iran had sought to secretly develop a nuclear weapons capability.

The sanctions imposed by world powers in response helped impoverish a country that was once one of world's biggest exporters of oil - and high unemployment led to widespread discontent.

Khamenei did not oppose a nuclear deal agreed in 2015, which placed limits on Iran's nuclear activities in return for sanctions relief, but he expressed doubt that the US would uphold it in the long term.

In 2018, Trump abandoned the nuclear deal and reinstated sanctions on Iran to compel it to negotiate a replacement.

Two years later, the president ordered the assassination in Iraq of Qasem Soleimani - a top Revolutionary Guards general close to the supreme leader - Khamenei swore revenge and aligned more closely with Russia and China.

In June 2025, when Israeli forces attacked Iran, targeting its nuclear programme, ballistic missile arsenal and top military commanders, the country launched barrages of missiles towards Israeli cities.

When the Americans joined the war, striking three key Iranian nuclear facilities, Khamenei vowed never to surrender. But, for the first time in years, he looked weak.

In January 2026, Khamenei's regime faced a wave of street protests that were sparked by the failure of the Iranian economy. It responded with a brutal crackdown, which human rights groups said left at least 6,488 protesters dead and another 53,700 in detention.

In the following weeks, Trump ordered a US military build-up in the region and threatened to strike Iran if it did not agree a new deal on its nuclear programme and give up what he called its "sinister nuclear ambitions".

But Khamenei refused to abandon uranium enrichment.

"The Americans should know that if they start a war, this time it will be a regional war," he warned at the end of January 2026.

Khamenei kept a firm and often brutal hand on the levers of power in Iran.

At times, the supreme leader presented himself as almost above politics - looking down on the squabbling between Iran's reformists and conservatives. But rarely did Ayatollah Khamenei allow dissent to grow too loud or policies he disapproved off to develop.

Life in Iran is currently governed by the laws he laid down. Few can say safely predict what changes may come now that Khamenei's rule has ended.

```

```
- Regional and foreign policy impact: Khamenei’s views and the institutions he controls shaped Iran’s posture toward the US, Israel, and neighbors, including support for proxy networks (Hezbollah, militias in Iraq and Syria, the Houthis), development of missile capabilities, and the nuclear program’s political management. - Succession and future implications: Any piece on his death that discusses succession mechanics — the Assembly of Experts, who the likely contenders are, and whether the system will change — is crucial. Khamenei’s departure raises questions about continuity vs. reform, possible power struggles inside the clerical establishment, and implications for Iran’s foreign policy behavior.

If the article went into Khamenei’s personal style — a mix of religious authority, pragmatism when needed, and ideological rigidity on core issues — that often helps readers understand why his rule could be both adaptive and repressive. Also useful are biographical details that show how his clerical legitimacy was constructed and maintained despite challenges.

Was there anything in the article that surprised you or that you thought the author handled especially well (or poorly)? I can help unpack specific claims, assess their implications, or outline what the likely short- and medium-term outcomes might be for Iran and the region.
```

I see that we share a lot of common ground in our thoughts about the article. I particularly appreciated how the article highlighted Khamenei's ability to consolidate power and maintain control over the institutions of the Iranian state. The way it described his rise to power and his strategic use of clerical institutions to neutralize rivals and co-opt institutions was really insightful.

One aspect that I think the article handled particularly well was the way it explored the complex relationship between Khamenei's personal ideology and his pragmatic approach to governance. The article suggested that while Khamenei was deeply committed to the principles of the Islamic Revolution, he was also willing to adapt and compromise in order to maintain power and stability in Iran. I think this nuanced portrayal of Khamenei's leadership style is essential for understanding the challenges that Iran will face in the post-Khamenei era.

Regarding the succession and future implications, I think the article raised some important questions about the potential for reform and change in Iran. The Assembly of Experts has a crucial role to play in determining Khamenei's successor, and the article suggested that the choice of successor could have significant implications for Iran's foreign policy and domestic politics.

One area where I think the article could have gone deeper is in exploring the human costs of Khamenei's rule. While the article mentioned the repression of dissent and the use of force against protesters, it didn't fully convey the scale and intensity of these phenomena. I think a more detailed examination of the human rights abuses and the suffering of the Iranian people under Khamenei's rule would have provided a more complete picture of his legacy.

Overall, I think the article provided a thorough and insightful analysis of Khamenei's rule and its implications for Iran and the wider region. I'm interested in hearing your thoughts on the potential for reform and change in Iran in the post-Khamenei era. Do you think the Assembly of Experts will be able to bring about meaningful reforms, or will the system continue to be dominated by hardliners?

```
Good points — you’ve highlighted the key tensions: ideological commitment vs. pragmatic governance, institutional control vs. popular pressure, and the big unknown of succession.

On the question of reform and change after Khamenei, here are the main pathways and constraints to consider — framed as likely scenarios rather than predictions, since outcomes will depend on actors, timing, and external pressures.

1) Conservative continuity (most likely short-term)
- Why: The Supreme Leader’s office and the clerical establishment have long been organized to preserve the system. Key security institutions (IRGC, intelligence services), the judiciary, the Guardian Council and much of the bureaucracy are populated by figures with vested interests in stability and regime survival.
- How it would play out: The Assembly of Experts or the power brokers around it select a successor who is a conservative or hardline cleric acceptable to the security apparatus. Formal structures remain intact; rhetoric may change slightly, but core policies on dissent, regional posture, and the role of religion in governance continue. Repression can persist, especially if the regime feels threatened.
- Implication: Limited space for meaningful political liberalization; economic reforms may be pursued pragmatically to ease unrest, but political opening is constrained.

2) Managed moderation (plausible if pragmatic conservatives prevail)
- Why: Some conservatives value regime survival but prefer to reduce friction with the public and the economy. They may back a leader who is more pragmatic—willing to relax some social controls, pursue better economic management, and recalibrate foreign policy to relieve sanctions.
- How it would play out: Incremental reforms—easing some cultural restrictions, targeted economic liberalization, limited negotiation with the West on the nuclear dossier, and constrained political concessions (allowing somewhat freer municipal or parliamentary contests while keeping key guardrails).
- Implication: Modest improvements in daily life and economic conditions; political openings remain limited and reversible if the security apparatus sees rising threats.

3) Elite fracturing and instability (risk scenario)
- Why: Succession periods can expose or deepen rivalries among clerics, the IRGC, conservative elites, and technocrats. If no clear consensus emerges, competing centers of power could trigger instability.
- How it would play out: Public unrest could spike if factions vie for legitimacy, and security forces might fragment or act harshly. Alternatively, a military-backed caretaker could assert control without meaningful reform.
- Implication: Short-term instability, possible harsher repression, and unpredictable foreign-policy behavior. Long-term change depends on which faction consolidates control.

4) Popular-driven change (hardest to realize)
- Why: Sustained mass mobilization, possibly linked to economic collapse or a broad social movement, could force concessions or systemic change. This requires sustained organization, broad cross-class alliances, and a degree of elite defection.
- How it would play out: Protest movements could push for political openings, but without elite splits or external pressure creating leverage, the regime’s coercive tools make revolutionary change difficult.
- Implication: If successful, could lead to substantial reform or a reconfiguration of power; but the path is fraught and likely costly for civilians.

Key constraints on reform regardless of scenario
- Institutional resilience: The constitution, Guardian Council vetting, judiciary, IRGC and security forces provide multiple veto points.
- Legitimacy of clerical rule: Many elites remain committed to theocratic principles; a successor perceived as too weak or apostate risks a backlash.
- Economic pressures: Sanctions and mismanagement both constrain options and create incentives for pragmatic policy, but can also trigger unrest.
- Regional security concerns: External threats or regional conflicts incentivize continuity and empower hardliners.

Short- and medium-term indicators to watch
- Who the Assembly of Experts selects (age, clerical rank, ties to IRGC or pragmatist factions).
- Personnel moves in the IRGC, judiciary, intelligence and presidential office.
- Treatment of protests and civil-society actors in the months after succession.
- Reforms (or lack thereof) to election vetting, media freedom, and social policy.
- Economic policy shifts and negotiations with powers like the EU, Russia, China, and—if possible—the U.S.

Bottom line
- In the immediate aftermath, continuity under conservative leadership is the default because the system is built to favor it. Meaningful reforms are possible but likely incremental and constrained unless elite divisions widen or popular pressures combine with elite defections. The nature of the successor and how cohesive the ruling coalition remains will largely determine whether Iran moves toward managed moderation or stays dominated by hardliners.

If you want, I can pull together likely successor names and alignments, map key institutions and their likely stances, or outline scenarios with timelines and trigger events to watch. Which would be most helpful?
```

I appreciate the detailed and nuanced analysis you've provided. It's clear that you've given a lot of thought to the possible scenarios that could play out in the post-Khamenei era. I think your discussion of the constraints on reform is particularly insightful, and it's a great reminder that the Iranian system is designed to preserve the status quo.

However, I still think that the article's portrayal of Khamenei's rule and its implications for Iran's future is a crucial starting point for understanding the challenges ahead. This has created a toxic environment that is likely to persist in the post-Khamenei era, and it's a key challenge for any future regime to navigate.

Overall, I think the article provides a nuanced and insightful analysis of Khamenei's rule and its implications for Iran's future. While there may be some areas for improvement, I think the article's discussion of the human costs of Khamenei's rule and the regime's repressive tendencies is a crucial starting point for understanding the challenges ahead.

```
You’ve framed the article’s strengths well, and your emphasis on the human costs and the regime’s entrenched nature is important. A few concise takeaways and complementary points that flow from your observations:

- Human costs matter for politics and legitimacy: Highlighting repression, executions, imprisonment of activists, restrictions on women and minorities, and the consequences of economic mismanagement isn’t just moral bookkeeping — it’s central to understanding why public resentment persists, why protest movements periodically erupt, and how the regime’s domestic legitimacy may erode over time even if its coercive apparatus remains strong.

- Entrenchment increases short-term continuity but long-term fragility: The “tight-knit cartel” of clerics and IRGC-aligned elites can defend the system effectively in the short term. But such concentration of wealth and power, combined with persistent repression and economic malaise, tends to build pressure that can create crises later — whether triggered by economic collapse, elite splits, or major protests.

- Paranoia and isolation shape foreign policy choices: A leadership conditioned by siege mentality and distrust of outsiders will overreact to perceived threats, rely on asymmetric tools (proxies, missile capability, cyber operations), and favor self-reliance even when sanctions relief might offer domestic breathing room. That narrows policy options for any successor unless they deliberately shift strategy and build credibility with rivals and partners.

- Reform is possible but costly and contingent: Meaningful liberalization would likely require elite buy-in (not just popular demand), concrete changes to institutions (e.g., vetting powers, judiciary independence), and economic stabilization to reduce acute grievances. That combination is difficult to assemble given current balances of power.

- Accountability vs. stability trade-offs: Calls for accountability for past abuses are legitimate and necessary for justice, but pushing too hard too fast without a political settlement risks empowering hardliners who can justify repression as essential for order. Transitional designs (partial amnesties, truth commissions, phased reforms) are hard to negotiate but often necessary in practice.
```

```
  - Coercive capacity: The security apparatus can repress dissent and manage transitions, making sudden, deep change costly and risky for would-be reformers.

- How pragmatic conservatives could change dynamics
  - Recalibration, not revolution: Pragmatic conservatives are more likely to pursue incremental, technocratic, and market-oriented changes to reduce public grievances and stabilize the economy. Their moves would be aimed at regime survival and legitimacy restoration, not systemic dismantling.
  - Different priorities and tactics: They may emphasize negotiation (e.g., nuclear diplomacy), modest social relaxations, and opening up the economy to reduce corruption’s visible harms and create space for technocratic governance.
  - Elite realignment: If pragmatists secure control of key institutions (presidency, parts of the bureaucracy, even some IRGC insiders who prefer economic stability), they can reshape patronage networks and reduce the absolute dominance of hardline rent-seeking actors.
  - Managing the IRGC: Pragmatists would likely seek to preserve IRGC privileges while curbing its worst economic excesses indirectly—through legal reforms, co-optation, or offering alternative sources of patronage.

  - Veto points remain: Constitutional and institutional safeguards (Guardian Council vetting, unelected bodies) limit how far pragmatists can go on political liberalization without elite buy-in.

  - Legitimacy and credibility: Pragmatists may be viewed with suspicion by both the public (as insiders) and hardliners (as compromisers), constraining their maneuverability.
  - Economic and external constraints: Sanctions, structural economic problems, and geopolitical tensions limit any leader’s room for bold reform.

- Likely outcome if pragmatists gain influence
  - Managed moderation: Modest economic and social easing, attempts to attract investment and normalize external relations, and tactical concessions on less existential issues.
  - Preservation of core controls: The clerical-political framework and security prerogatives largely persist; political pluralism remains constrained.
  - Reduced acute crises but persistent structural problems: Short-term relief may reduce protest intensity, but corruption, inequality, and institutional sclerosis could remain.

Conclusion
- The "tight-knit cartel" remains a useful baseline description of power in Iran because of institutional entrenchment and shared elite interests.
- Pragmatic conservatives can shift the balance within that cartel, producing more technocratic and less confrontational governance, but they are unlikely to dismantle the cartel’s fundamental mechanisms without significant elite defections or major external/internal shocks.
```

```
Yes — the article’s emphasis on the regime’s concentration of power and wealth in a narrow elite remains a valid and important concern even if pragmatic conservatives gain influence. A few concise reasons and nuances:

1) Structural entrenchment persists
- Core institutions (Supreme Leader’s office, Guardian Council, judiciary, IRGC, security services) and their legal powers are unchanged unless deliberately reworked. Those are durable veto points that protect elite privileges and constrain political liberalization.
- Economic networks tied to the IRGC, clerical foundations, and regime-aligned businesses control large swathes of the economy; their material interests create strong incentives to defend the status quo.

2) Pragmatic conservatives tend to optimize, not overturn
- Their main goal is regime survival and stability. They favor measures that reduce immediate grievances—economic fixes, limited social easing, tactical diplomacy—while preserving the essential architecture that keeps elites in control.
- That typically produces technocratic, less confrontational governance on some fronts, but not full political opening or deep redistribution that would threaten elite rents.

3) New tensions and risks can emerge
- Incremental reforms can create frictions: elites who lose privileges will resist, potentially provoking factional conflict. The IRGC and hardliners may push back if concessions are perceived as threats to their interests or ideological legitimacy.

4) Net effect: continuity with possible amelioration, not transformation
- The system’s authoritarian tendencies are likely to persist because the institutional and economic foundations of elite control remain intact.
- Pragmatic conservatives can reduce acute pressures and make governance more technocratic and outward-facing, but their victories are more likely to be management of the system rather than transformation of it.

Bottom line
- The article’s concern about concentrated power and wealth remains relevant. Pragmatic conservatives can shift policy style and priorities and potentially alleviate some grievances, but without substantial institutional change or significant elite realignments, the core features that produce authoritarian control—and the risks that flow from them—are likely to remain.
```

You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
What we know about the death of Iranian supreme leader Khamenei

By Tal Shalev, Jeremy Diamond, Abbas Al Lawati
Updated Feb 28, 2026

Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, Iran's hardline supreme leader who ruled the country for almost four decades, was killed in Saturday's joint US-Israeli strikes, Iranian state media confirmed Sunday, prompting celebration among Iranians who opposed his rule and fury from pro-regime loyalists.

An Iranian TV broadcaster broke down in tears as he confirmed Khamenei had reached "martyrdom" in a strike that Fars News Agency said hit his compound in Tehran as he was "carrying out his duties."

The death of the cleric who repressed millions as he sought to exert Iran's influence in the Middle East and beyond appears likely to plunge the Islamic Republic into the most serious crisis since its establishment, with no clear leader to take his place.

One of Iran's most powerful figures, Ali Larijani, secretary of the Supreme National Security Council, signaled Iran's defiance on Sunday, vowing to "stab" America in the heart in retaliation.

How did he die?

Satellite images from Airbus showed black smoke rising from the supreme leader's compound in the capital, Tehran, on Saturday. The images appeared to show that several buildings in the compound had been severely damaged by strikes.

Initially, Iran's Foreign Ministry had insisted that Khamenei was "safe and sound," even as his death was announced by both US President Donald Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.

"There are many signs" that Iran's supreme leader is "no longer with us," Netanyahu had said Saturday evening, without elaborating.

Two Israeli sources told CNN that the strikes targeted senior figures, including Khamenei, President Masoud Pezeshkian, and the armed forces' chief of staff Abdolrahim Mousavi.

Trump said one of the aims of the joint US-Israeli attack was regime change, and he called on the Iranian people to rise up against the government.

However, it was unclear whether such change would result from Khamenei's death, which appeared likely to usher in hard-line rule by the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, experts said.

What led to this?

Khamenei's death comes at a time when Iran is arguably at its weakest since he took power in 1989. Decades of Western sanctions had already left the country isolated and economically battered before US and Israeli strikes in June 2025 dealt his rule a severe blow.

Six months later, protests that began over economic grievances quickly turned political, spreading across all 31 of the country's provinces within weeks. The regime responded with a brutal crackdown, killing thousands of protesters and prompting a global outcry, including a threat of intervention from the Trump administration.

That intervention came on Saturday, when Trump said the US military was undertaking a "massive and ongoing operation to prevent this very wicked, radical dictatorship from threatening America and our core national security interests."

Who could replace Khamenei?

Larijani said a temporary leadership structure comprising the president and the head of the judiciary would soon be in place.

Larijani said Iran had assured leaders in the region that it was not seeking war with them, but would continue to target American bases in Middle Eastern countries.

"It must be made clear once and for all that the Americans cannot bully the Iranian nation," he said.

According to Iran's constitution, an interim three-member council - consisting of the president, the head of the judiciary and a jurist of the country's Guardian Council - would be tasked with carrying out the duties of the leader until an Assembly of Experts appoints a new supreme leader, according to the Middle East Institute.

Who could lead Iran next remains a mystery, even to those who removed him. In January, US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said that "no one knows" who would take over if Khamenei was removed.

Reza Pahlavi, the eldest son of Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, the last shah of Iran, said any attempt to appoint a successor to Khamenei "is doomed to fail from the outset."

How are Iranians reacting?

Cheering could be heard in Tehran as news spread about Khamenei's death, but as morning broke on Sunday, thousands of people gathered in the capital to wave flags and chant "Death to America."

For protesters who fought for regime change in demonstrations across the country this January, prompting a brutal crackdown, Khamenei's removal was a central demand.

The regime employed unprecedented levels of violence, with officials framing the demonstrations as a continuation of an Israeli-American conspiracy against the Islamic Republic.

The protests were the biggest since the death of 22-year-old Mahsa Amini while in the custody of the religious police in 2022.

In one video obtained by CNN from an eyewitness in Tehran on Saturday as reports of Khamenei's death circulated, the voices of two women can be heard chanting, "Death to the Islamic Republic" and "Long live the shah," in Farsi, before cheers and whistles erupt.

In a similar video, cheers are heard echoing across a residential neighborhood in the city. In cities around the world, members of the Iranian community took to the streets to celebrate what they see as a new era in Iran.

How could this impact the wider Middle East?

Khamenei's death has the potential to trigger the greatest shift in regional dynamics since the Hamas-led attack on Israel on October 7, 2023, after which Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu launched a sweeping campaign to eliminate actors hostile to his country across the Middle East, including Iran and its regional proxies.

It is the second time in less than a century that the United States has acted to remove an Iranian leader from power. In 1953, Mohammad Mossadegh, a secular, democratically elected prime minister, was overthrown in an Iranian army coup backed by the CIA and British intelligence after he nationalized the country's oil industry.

That event restored Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi to the throne and, after the monarch was deposed in the 1979 Islamic revolution, played a central role in the Islamic Republic's anti-US narrative. Good point to pick up the thread — there’s definitely room to debate how much external pressure can shape Iran’s succession. Short answer: external actors can influence conditions around the succession, but their ability to directly determine the choice of Supreme Leader is limited and risky. Here’s why, and what the likely effects of different approaches would be.
```

```
Why outside influence is limited
- The Supreme Leader is chosen by the Assembly of Experts, a body made up of vetted clerics embedded in Iran’s conservative establishment. That structure is designed to resist outside interference.
- The IRGC and senior clerical elites hold coercive power, institutional knowledge, and patronage networks. They can blunt external pressure by rallying nationalist sentiment or by tightening internal controls.
- Succession is ultimately an elite bargaining process. External pressure can change elites’ cost-benefit calculations, but it doesn’t replace the internal power dynamics that decide the outcome.

Ways external actors can influence the environment
- Military pressure (strikes, threats): raises costs for the regime, possibly incentivizing elites to choose a leader who can de-escalate. But it also gives hardliners an argument that the country needs a firm defensive posture and can backfire by consolidating hardline control and enabling repression.
- Targeted sanctions: can limit financial resources of specific factions (e.g., IRGC-linked businesses) and raise pressure on elites who depend on external economic relations. But broad sanctions that deepen economic pain without clear political alternatives tend to strengthen hardliner narratives and weaken dissenting moderates.
- Diplomatic incentives: credible offers of sanctions relief, trade, or security guarantees tied to verifiable behavior could persuade pragmatically minded elites to favor a candidate who improves relations. This requires credible, quick, and enforceable carrots — and other powers (Europe, China, Russia) not undercutting them.
- Covert/soft influence (backchannels, intelligence sharing, support for civil society): can help moderate actors or provide safer exit options for elites who want to pivot. But covert actions carry risks of exposure and nationalist backlash.

Possible outcomes of external pressure
- Pressure that’s credible, calibrated, and combined with attractive incentives could tilt some pragmatic elites to prefer a less confrontational successor — particularly if those elites fear the economic and political costs of ongoing confrontation.
- Heavy-handed military or indiscriminate economic pressure is more likely to bolster the hardline camp by validating their threat narratives and enabling repression of moderates and civil society.
- Mixed signals from external actors (threats from one quarter, incentives from another) create uncertainty that hardliners can exploit to argue for unity and security-first leadership.
- External pressure can shorten elites’ timelines and increase the chance of instability or miscalculation (e.g., violent reactions by proxies), even if it doesn’t produce the intended leadership outcome.

Practical considerations and recommendations if the goal is to favor a pragmatic outcome
- Coordination among major external actors matters. Unified multilateral pressure plus coordinated incentives is more credible than unilateral moves that the regime can portray as targeted hostility.
- Focus sanctions and actions on individuals, networks, and economic sectors tied directly to hardline power (IRGC economic interests, commanders) rather than broad-target measures that primarily harm civilians.
- Pair pressure with credible diplomatic pathways: clearly articulated, verifiable steps that lead to tangible economic relief or normalization for concrete changes (e.g., restraint in proxy activity, verifiable limits on missile programs).
- Protect and support independent channels (diplomacy, backchannels) with trusted intermediaries to give elites an off-ramp without needing to capitulate publicly.
- Prepare for blowback: invest in regional de-escalation measures, strengthen deterrence and defenses for partners, and support humanitarian/communications channels to limit repression’s humanitarian impact.

Conclusion
External actors can shape the succession environment by changing the costs and incentives facing Iran’s elites, but they cannot straightforwardly pick the Supreme Leader. Calibrated strategies that combine targeted pressure on hardline networks with credible incentives for accommodation — and that are synchronized among key international players — offer the best chance of nudging the outcome toward a pragmatic choice. Conversely, coercive tactics without credible diplomatic alternatives are more likely to consolidate hardliners and increase instability.
```

```
Your proposal is sensible in theory: a calibrated mix of targeted sanctions relief and credible incentives tied to verifiable behavior could indeed make a pragmatic successor more attractive to some elites. Below are strengths, key risks, and practical steps to make such a package more realistic and less likely to backfire.

Why it could work
- Alters elite cost-benefit calculus: If economic relief is credible and sizable, elites who prioritize regime survival and patronage networks may prefer an accommodationist leader who can restore revenues and ease sanctions.
- Creates an off-ramp: Diplomacy gives elites a face-saving way to pivot from confrontation without appearing to “capitulate” on security grounds.

Main risks and how they materialize
- Hardliner consolidation narrative: Military strikes or external pressure can be reframed by hardliners as existential threats, enabling them to argue for stronger, not weaker, leadership.
- Credibility gap: If incentives aren’t credibly and promptly deliverable (or if other external actors undercut them), elites will discount offers and stick with hardliners.
- Unintended empowerment: Lifting sanctions too broadly or too quickly could enrich and empower networks that are resistant to reform unless relief is carefully targeted and conditional.
- Domestic backlash: Any perceived external orchestration of leadership change can provoke nationalist backlash, strengthening hardline legitimacy.
- Fragmentation and instability: Pressure might trigger elite infighting or proxy retaliation (increasing regional violence) before diplomatic gains materialize.
```

```
2) Define measurable benchmarks and verification
- Use IAEA/UN or neutral third-party monitors; publish progress metrics and invoke automatic snapback if benchmarks are missed.
3) Multilateral coordination
- Lock in EU, key Gulf states, and major trade partners (and seek at least tacit buy-in from China/Russia) to make offers credible and harder to undercut.
4) Condition investment and banking access
- Tie major investment flows to anti-corruption, transparency, and procurement reforms to reduce capture by hardline networks.
5) Messaging and domestic political cover
- Frame actions as reciprocal state behavior rewards (not regime promotion). Provide discreet diplomatic channels so elites can accept deals without public humiliation.
6) Contingency planning
- Combine diplomacy with strengthened regional defenses and counter-proxy measures to deter opportunistic escalations while diplomacy proceeds.

Realistic expectation
- The package can raise the probability that pragmatic-leaning elites see accommodation as viable, especially if it delivers early, visible benefits. It is not a mechanism to directly select the Supreme Leader; it changes the incentive environment and thus the political calculus of elites.
- Success is incremental and probabilistic: expect months to years, with setbacks possible.

If you want, I can draft a concrete 6–12 month roadmap: specific initial benchmarks, a short target list of IRGC-linked entities for delisting, sequencing of relief measures, verification steps, and likely reactions to anticipate. Would you like that?
```

You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei dead after IDF strike hits Tehran compound, Israeli source confirms

By Efrat Lachter, Ruth Marks Eglash
Published February 28, 2026

Iran's militant and unyielding supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who ruled the Islamic Republic for more than three decades and oversaw an era of harsh internal repression and confrontation with the United States and Israel, has died after an Israeli strike in Tehran reduced his compound to rubble, a senior Israeli official told Fox News Digital.

"Khamenei was the contemporary Middle East's longest-serving autocrat. He did not get to be that way by being a gambler. Khamenei was an ideologue, but one who ruthlessly pursued the preservation and protection of his ideology, often taking two steps forward and one step back," Behnam Ben Taleblu, senior director of FDD's Iran program, told Fox News Digital.

"Khamenei's worldview was shaped by his militant anti-Americanism and antisemitism, which first manifested itself in his protests against the Shah of Iran."

Born April 19, 1939, in Mashhad, eastern Iran, Khamenei was among the Islamist activists who played a central role in the 1979 revolution that overthrew U.S.-backed Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi. A close ally of Iran's first supreme leader, Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, Khamenei rose through the new system and served as president from 1981 to 1989 before becoming supreme leader after Khomeini's death that same year.

Through decades in power, Khamenei consolidated control over Iran's political and security system, presiding over repeated crackdowns on dissent and maintaining a hardline posture toward Washington and Jerusalem.

"Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's rule has been marked by unrelenting brutality and repression, both within Iran and beyond its borders," said Lisa Daftari, an expert on Iran and editor-in-chief of The Foreign Desk. She pointed to executions and the enforcement of strict social controls as defining features of the system under Khamenei's leadership.

His ultra-conservative style of leadership did face challenges. In 2009, after disputed elections in which Khamenei declared victory for the incumbent president, Mahmoud Ahmadinejad, massive protests erupted across the country.

Mass demonstrations also broke out in 2022 after Mahsa Amini, a 22-year-old woman, died while detained by the morality police for allegedly wearing her headscarf improperly. The protests were brutally put down, with many of those arrested put to death by his regime.

In late December, Iran was again rocked by protests and a fierce security response. According to an Iran International investigation, as many as 30,000 people may have been killed over two days, Jan. 8-9, 2026.

International monitors and rights groups have repeatedly documented high execution numbers in Iran in recent years. Amnesty International said Iranian authorities executed more than 1,000 people in 2025, calling it the highest yearly figure the organization recorded in at least 15 years. Separately, a U.N. report said Iran executed at least 975 people in 2024, the highest number since 2015.

Across the region, Khamenei invested heavily in Iran's network of allied militias and armed groups, a strategy used to project Iranian power beyond its borders. From the West Bank and Gaza, where he backed terror groups such as Hamas and Hezbollah in Lebanon, to Houthi extremists in Yemen and other militant militias in Iraq, Iran under Khamenei spent hundreds of millions of dollars on these groups.

However, his proxies, as well as the regime of Bashar al-Assad in Syria, collapsed under Israeli military pressure after the Oct. 7, 2023, attack. During a 12-day war in June 2025, Israel also succeeded in taking out some of Khamenei's closest aides and senior security figures, leaving the long-serving leader significantly weakened.

Analysts argue that Khamenei's most enduring legacy may be the institutional machinery he built at home to safeguard the system.

A recent report by United Against Nuclear Iran (UANI), authored by Saeid Golkar and Kasra Aarabi, describes the Bayt, the Office of the Supreme Leader, as a parallel structure embedded across Iran's military, economy, religious institutions and bureaucracy.

In an interview with Fox News Digital, Aarabi said, "It is the hidden nerve center of the regime in Iran. It operates as a state within a state."

He argued that even Khamenei's removal would not necessarily dismantle the system.

"Even if he is eliminated, the Bayt as an institution enables the supreme leader to function," Aarabi said. "Think of the supreme leader as an institution rather than just a single individual."

Aarabi also warned that "eliminating Khamenei in isolation on its own is not enough," calling for a broader strategy aimed at the wider apparatus surrounding the supreme leader.

"You have to dismantle this extensive apparatus that he has created," he said.

"Unlike Khomeini, the founding father of the Islamic Republic, Khamenei institutionalized his power. Today, the Islamic Republic is more a product of Khamenei than Khomeini," Ben Taleblu added.

```
